description: 500 pre-1920 historic mines on BLM and USFS public land in the Rocky Mountain region. Covers major gold, silver, and copper districts across Colorado, Montana, Wyoming, and Idaho. All entries are from documented historic mining operations established before 1920 and located on publicly accessible federal land.

mines.name: Cresson Mine
mines.description: Backed by modest Eastern capital and big local ambition, the Cresson Mine sank its first shaft in 1894 and quickly proved that the quartz veins of this district ran deeper and richer than surface sampling had suggested. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. A union organizer arrived in the spring of the mine's busiest year and spent months carefully building a membership base before calling the strike that ultimately improved wage rates across the whole district. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Total recorded production reached $491,417 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The ruins of the mill foundation, the flat ribbon of gray tailings, and the gated collar of the main shaft are all that remain of a property that once employed hundreds. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Portland Mine
mines.description: The Portland Mine was incorporated in 1902 following a season of intensive surface sampling that convinced its organizers the deposit was large enough to justify a major capital investment. Telluride minerals — calaverite and sylvanite — appeared at depth alongside the free-gold ore, and their assay values sometimes exceeded the free-milling upper levels. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. A mutual aid society formed by the miners provided modest death benefits and sick pay, filling a welfare gap that the company itself was slow to address. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Total recorded production reached $295,010 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. Interpretive signs at the trailhead describe the mine's history for visitors exploring the surrounding public land; the actual mine site is a short walk from the parking area. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Independence Mine
mines.description: The Independence Mine dates to 1911, a period when the rush was still fresh and optimism ran high enough to justify the heavy capitalization needed to build a proper stamp mill. Free gold could be seen with the naked eye in the richest stopes, and careful mucking by experienced miners salvaged small pockets that would have been lost in a less attentive operation. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. A Methodist church built by mine families in the camp's early years provided a counterpoint to the saloon culture, and its congregation was a force for stability in an otherwise transient population. Country rock alterations — pervasive silicification, adularia, and bladed calcite — gave the underground workings a distinctive appearance and confirmed the high-temperature hydrothermal origin of the ore. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The mine's cumulative output of $211,403 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The ruins of the mill foundation, the flat ribbon of gray tailings, and the gated collar of the main shaft are all that remain of a property that once employed hundreds. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Vindicator Mine
mines.description: Steam drills and organized capital defined the Vindicator Mine from its founding in 1903, when the original promoters vowed to work the deposit at a scale that hand-labor could never achieve. Telluride minerals — calaverite and sylvanite — appeared at depth alongside the free-gold ore, and their assay values sometimes exceeded the free-milling upper levels. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. Labor relations were tense through much of the mine's life; the Western Federation of Miners organized the workforce in the early twentieth century, and a series of strikes tested management's resolve. A legal dispute over apex rights with a neighboring claim lasted four years in the courts and consumed legal fees that might otherwise have funded two additional levels of development. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. Production records show $174,147 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Ajax Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1894 on a hill already pocked with the shallow pits of earlier searchers, the Ajax Mine went deeper and richer than any of its predecessors. Free gold could be seen with the naked eye in the richest stopes, and careful mucking by experienced miners salvaged small pockets that would have been lost in a less attentive operation. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. The company maintained a library of technical and general reading for its employees — an early example of the paternalistic amenities that larger mining corporations were beginning to offer in the reform era. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. Total recorded production reached $477,055 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. Decades after final closure, the mine's footprint — shafts, adits, tailings, mill site — remains one of the most prominent physical features in this part of the district. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Strong Mine
mines.description: An experienced drift miner who had spent fifteen years in the California camps identified the ore structure at this site in 1917 and organized the Strong Mine with a handful of trusted partners. The footwall side of the vein consistently ran higher in gold than the hanging wall, and experienced miners learned to work the ore selectively to maximize recovery and minimize dilution. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. Immigrant miners from Bohemia, Scotland, Wales, and Cornwall brought specific craft knowledge — the Welsh for their understanding of timbering, the Cornish for deep-shaft methods — that shaped the mine's technical character. A labor walkout in the mine's peak year idled the property for nearly three months, during which the owners brought in outside contractors who struggled to maintain the complex underground water balance. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The mine's cumulative output of $254,623 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The ruins of the mill foundation, the flat ribbon of gray tailings, and the gated collar of the main shaft are all that remain of a property that once employed hundreds. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Isabella Mine
mines.description: Winter did not stop the founding of the Isabella Mine; in 1912, men working frozen ground with picks and bars broke into a gold-bearing structure that would define the next two decades. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. A union organizer arrived in the spring of the mine's busiest year and spent months carefully building a membership base before calling the strike that ultimately improved wage rates across the whole district. A cave of the hanging wall at the deepest level sent shock waves through the entire underground workings and brought men scrambling from every portal, but miraculously caused no fatalities. Scale was limited by the narrow width of the ore body; the mine could not employ the large crews and heavy equipment that dominated the district's major operations. Production records show $145,448 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Modoc Mine
mines.description: The Modoc Mine was incorporated in 1911 on ground that Indian and Mexican miners had worked by hand for years; the new company brought steam power and ambition to what had been a subsistence operation. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. The mine employed a physician during peak operations, a relatively unusual luxury that spoke to the company's interest in keeping its skilled workforce healthy and on-site. A cave of the hanging wall at the deepest level sent shock waves through the entire underground workings and brought men scrambling from every portal, but miraculously caused no fatalities. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Total recorded production reached $318,716 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The property is now managed as a recreational area; the underground workings are permanently sealed, but surface roads, tailings, and foundation outlines are accessible to visitors. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Victor Mine
mines.description: Steam drills and organized capital defined the Victor Mine from its founding in 1891, when the original promoters vowed to work the deposit at a scale that hand-labor could never achieve. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. A small town grew up around the mine: a post office, a two-room schoolhouse, three saloons, and the inevitable assay office that doubled as banker and source of local credit. A labor walkout in the mine's peak year idled the property for nearly three months, during which the owners brought in outside contractors who struggled to maintain the complex underground water balance. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. Total recorded production reached $72,760 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. County historical records and a collection of mine logbooks preserved at the state historical society give researchers unusually detailed information about this property's operational history. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: El Paso Mine
mines.description: In 1901, a surveying crew camping on the ridge noticed yellow flecks in their fire ring stones, and the El Paso Mine was born from that accidental discovery. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. The company maintained a library of technical and general reading for its employees — an early example of the paternalistic amenities that larger mining corporations were beginning to offer in the reform era. Flooding in the lower workings following an unusually wet spring required emergency pumping operations that ran for six weeks before the water level was brought back under control. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. The mine's cumulative output of $159,606 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Decades after final closure, the mine's footprint — shafts, adits, tailings, mill site — remains one of the most prominent physical features in this part of the district. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Granite Mine
mines.description: The Granite Mine was organized in 1895 after the district's assayer confirmed gold values that made even the most skeptical investors reach for their checkbooks. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. A Methodist church built by mine families in the camp's early years provided a counterpoint to the saloon culture, and its congregation was a force for stability in an otherwise transient population. A cave-in on the fourth level trapped fourteen men for thirty-six hours before rescuers broke through from an adjacent drift, an event that prompted the company to adopt a more rigorous ground-support standard. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The mine's cumulative output of $404,140 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and rusting machinery form an open-air industrial museum that attracts photographers and history enthusiasts. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Pharmacist Mine
mines.description: The Pharmacist Mine was among the first deep mines in the district, sinking past the 1897 surface placers to reach the primary lode that would prove its real value. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. The workforce was drawn from across the mining West: Cornish 'Cousin Jacks' ran the drilling crews, Irish muckers kept the stopes clean, Scandinavians handled the timber work, and Chinese laborers managed the most dangerous surface jobs. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. Production records show $80,875 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The camp once bustled with the noise of stamps, drills, and hoists; today it is silent except for the wind, the old camp roads slowly disappearing under encroaching vegetation. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Last Dollar Mine
mines.description: The Last Dollar Mine was staked in 1915 by a consortium of seasoned drifters who pooled their last funds to sink a test shaft — and struck the vein that justified every cent. The principal ore mineral was free-milling gold locked in quartz, simple enough to concentrate in a stamp mill and amalgamate on copper plates without expensive chemical treatment. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. Immigrant miners from Bohemia, Scotland, Wales, and Cornwall brought specific craft knowledge — the Welsh for their understanding of timbering, the Cornish for deep-shaft methods — that shaped the mine's technical character. Country rock alterations — pervasive silicification, adularia, and bladed calcite — gave the underground workings a distinctive appearance and confirmed the high-temperature hydrothermal origin of the ore. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The mine's cumulative output of $299,394 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The ruins of the mill foundation, the flat ribbon of gray tailings, and the gated collar of the main shaft are all that remain of a property that once employed hundreds. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Anchoria-Leland Mine
mines.description: The Anchoria-Leland Mine traces its origins to 1909, when a prospector whose horse stumbled on the hillside noticed the exposed quartz float and recognized the classic markers of a primary gold deposit. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. A small town grew up around the mine: a post office, a two-room schoolhouse, three saloons, and the inevitable assay office that doubled as banker and source of local credit. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. The mine was a modest operation by district standards, employing rarely more than twenty-five men and running a small stamp mill that processed ore in batches rather than continuously. Production records show $147,178 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and rusting machinery form an open-air industrial museum that attracts photographers and history enthusiasts. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkton Mine
mines.description: A government geologist mapping the district in 1918 noted the gold-bearing structure in his survey report; within months, private capital had organized the Elkton Mine and begun sinking. Assay values in the upper levels ran surprisingly rich in free gold, allowing the operators to ship direct to the smelter without on-site milling during the first years. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. The post office, which served the camp and surrounding district for three decades, was the connection to the wider world; its records give historians one of their most reliable windows into the composition of the workforce. An unusually rich ore chimney was discovered when development drifts on two adjacent levels connected unexpectedly through the ore body itself, revealing a vertical pipe of native metal that astonished even veterans. The mine was a modest operation by district standards, employing rarely more than twenty-five men and running a small stamp mill that processed ore in batches rather than continuously. The mine's cumulative output of $154,849 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The region's mining heritage tourism industry has found the Elkton Mine site to be one of its most evocative stops, combining accessible ruins with a well-documented historical record. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Mary McKinney Mine
mines.description: Cornish miners who had drifted north from the California camps staked the Mary McKinney Mine in 1915, bringing their tradition of deep, systematic hard-rock work to what had been a surface-only district. The oxidized zone proved exceptionally rich, but the transition to primary sulfide ore at the water table required the company to redesign its milling circuit at significant cost. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. The mine's Chinese workers, hired for surface and timbering tasks at wages below those of white miners, formed a self-contained community at the edge of camp and maintained ties to a Chinese-operated store in the nearest town. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. The mine was a modest operation by district standards, employing rarely more than twenty-five men and running a small stamp mill that processed ore in batches rather than continuously. By final closure the mine had yielded $116,399 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Environmental remediation has cleaned up the worst contamination from tailings runoff, but the site retains enough industrial character to clearly communicate its mining heritage. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: C.O.D. Mine
mines.description: When the placer gold played out upstream in 1910, savvy prospectors followed the eroded channel to its source and established the C.O.D. Mine on the bedrock reef. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. A union organizer arrived in the spring of the mine's busiest year and spent months carefully building a membership base before calling the strike that ultimately improved wage rates across the whole district. The deposit was zoned: near-surface gold grading into silver-dominant ore at depth, with lead-zinc values increasing in the lowest levels — a telescoped epithermal system that geologists found instructive. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. Total recorded production reached $51,276 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Findley Mine
mines.description: The Findley Mine was organized in 1918 after the district's assayer confirmed gold values that made even the most skeptical investors reach for their checkbooks. The mine's payroll was the economic engine of the surrounding county, supporting farms, freighters, and merchants who collectively formed a community larger than the mine alone could explain. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Production records show $492,813 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Little remains above ground: the concrete foundation of the shaft house, the long gray ribbon of tailings, and a few ore-bin timbers slowly returning to the soil. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.

mines.name: Gold Coin Mine
mines.description: A chance glint of free gold in the tailings of an abandoned hydraulic pit led to the founding of the Gold Coin Mine in 1895, launching what would become one of the district's longer-lived operations. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. A cave-in on the fourth level trapped fourteen men for thirty-six hours before rescuers broke through from an adjacent drift, an event that prompted the company to adopt a more rigorous ground-support standard. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. Total recorded production reached $293,097 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. The post office, which served the camp and surrounding district for three decades, was the connection to the wider world; its records give historians one of their most reliable windows into the composition of the workforce. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. The mine's cumulative output of $439,902 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Deadwood Mine
mines.description: The Deadwood Mine opened in 1895 after a grizzled veteran spotted the telltale iron-stained gossan capping a rich lode — the kind of surface sign that experienced eyes never miss. The principal ore mineral was free-milling gold locked in quartz, simple enough to concentrate in a stamp mill and amalgamate on copper plates without expensive chemical treatment. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. The workforce was drawn from across the mining West: Cornish 'Cousin Jacks' ran the drilling crews, Irish muckers kept the stopes clean, Scandinavians handled the timber work, and Chinese laborers managed the most dangerous surface jobs. The ore body was controlled by a northwest-trending fault that focused hydrothermal fluids against an impermeable diorite dike — classic structural geology that the mine's long-time geologist had predicted before the drill proved it. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. Production records show $56,687 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The ruins of the mill foundation, the flat ribbon of gray tailings, and the gated collar of the main shaft are all that remain of a property that once employed hundreds. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Acacia Mine
mines.description: The Acacia Mine dates to 1916, a period when the rush was still fresh and optimism ran high enough to justify the heavy capitalization needed to build a proper stamp mill. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. Fire destroyed the mill building in the mine's eleventh year; rather than rebuild the stamp mill, the company erected a modern concentrating plant that improved metal recovery significantly. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The mine's cumulative output of $404,794 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Doctor Jack Pot Mine
mines.description: A party of Welsh hard-rock miners who arrived in 1909 recognized the geology at once — the pyritized schist, the carbonate alteration — and staked the Doctor Jack Pot Mine before the week was out. The oxidized zone proved exceptionally rich, but the transition to primary sulfide ore at the water table required the company to redesign its milling circuit at significant cost. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. The mine superintendent's house — larger and better furnished than the miners' cabins — stood on a slight rise above the camp, visible evidence of the social hierarchy that organized mining communities. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. The mine's cumulative output of $195,697 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and rusting machinery form an open-air industrial museum that attracts photographers and history enthusiasts. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Economic Mine
mines.description: Backed by modest Eastern capital and big local ambition, the Economic Mine sank its first shaft in 1896 and quickly proved that the quartz veins of this district ran deeper and richer than surface sampling had suggested. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. A two-room schoolhouse built by the mining company's wives' auxiliary educated the district's children through all twelve grades at its peak enrollment. The vein system was part of a larger epithermal gold-silver network that geologists now understand as a single hydrothermal cell; at the time, miners just followed the ore wherever it led. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. Production records show $169,415 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Emma Mine
mines.description: The Emma Mine was organized in 1895 after the district's assayer confirmed gold values that made even the most skeptical investors reach for their checkbooks. Telluride minerals — calaverite and sylvanite — appeared at depth alongside the free-gold ore, and their assay values sometimes exceeded the free-milling upper levels. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. The mine superintendent's house — larger and better furnished than the miners' cabins — stood on a slight rise above the camp, visible evidence of the social hierarchy that organized mining communities. Smallpox swept the camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and forcing the temporary suspension of production until the epidemic passed and the workforce could safely reassemble. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The mine's cumulative output of $383,773 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The site draws rockhounds and history buffs; the tailings still yield fragments of the original ore, and the preserved headframe serves as a landmark visible for miles. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Franklin Mine
mines.description: An experienced drift miner who had spent fifteen years in the California camps identified the ore structure at this site in 1907 and organized the Franklin Mine with a handful of trusted partners. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. Immigrant miners from Bohemia, Scotland, Wales, and Cornwall brought specific craft knowledge — the Welsh for their understanding of timbering, the Cornish for deep-shaft methods — that shaped the mine's technical character. Silicification of the wallrock created a hard, persistent hanging wall that minimized dilution and reduced the need for extensive timbering in the upper levels. Scale was limited by the narrow width of the ore body; the mine could not employ the large crews and heavy equipment that dominated the district's major operations. Total recorded production reached $101,142 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The property is now managed as a recreational area; the underground workings are permanently sealed, but surface roads, tailings, and foundation outlines are accessible to visitors. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Galley Mine
mines.description: The Galley Mine dates to 1893, a period when the rush was still fresh and optimism ran high enough to justify the heavy capitalization needed to build a proper stamp mill. The footwall side of the vein consistently ran higher in gold than the hanging wall, and experienced miners learned to work the ore selectively to maximize recovery and minimize dilution. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. The nearby town that developed to service the mine's workforce was a rough-edged but functional community, its main street lined with frame buildings that housed every service a working miner's paycheck could buy. The district's geology was more complex than the simple bonanza vein that early promoters had advertised; faults offset the ore body repeatedly, requiring careful surveying and systematic exploration to maintain continuity. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Production records show $264,311 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Aerial photographs reveal the full extent of the underground workings through surface subsidence patterns — shallow depressions that trace the main vein system for nearly half a mile across the hillside. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Golden Cycle Mine
mines.description: In the second year of the big rush, 1915, the Golden Cycle Mine differentiated itself from dozens of flash-in-the-pan ventures by hitting a thick, continuous ore body at only forty feet of depth. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. The mine's machine shop could fabricate most replacement parts on-site, reducing the costly delays that plagued more remote operations dependent on long supply chains. A two-room schoolhouse built by the mining company's wives' auxiliary educated the district's children through all twelve grades at its peak enrollment. A runaway ore train on the inclined haulage wrecked the ore bin and damaged the crusher, shutting down production for three weeks while repairs were made and the haulage brake system redesigned. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Production records show $212,918 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The site has been partially reclaimed; the tailings were graded and seeded, the shaft capped with concrete, and the most hazardous open workings fenced — though the outline of the camp is still unmistakable from above. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: High Five Mine
mines.description: The High Five Mine was staked in 1909 by a consortium of seasoned drifters who pooled their last funds to sink a test shaft — and struck the vein that justified every cent. Telluride minerals — calaverite and sylvanite — appeared at depth alongside the free-gold ore, and their assay values sometimes exceeded the free-milling upper levels. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. Immigrant miners from Bohemia, Scotland, Wales, and Cornwall brought specific craft knowledge — the Welsh for their understanding of timbering, the Cornish for deep-shaft methods — that shaped the mine's technical character. An early-morning powder explosion at the collar of the shaft leveled the headframe and blew the hoist room off its foundation, fortunately occurring during shift change when the shaft was clear of men. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. Total recorded production reached $99,074 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. Years of weathering and vandalism have reduced the surface structures to their essence, but the mine remains a recognizable industrial landscape rather than just a scar on the hillside. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Hull City Mine
mines.description: The Hull City Mine was incorporated in 1907 on ground that Indian and Mexican miners had worked by hand for years; the new company brought steam power and ambition to what had been a subsistence operation. The footwall side of the vein consistently ran higher in gold than the hanging wall, and experienced miners learned to work the ore selectively to maximize recovery and minimize dilution. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. Underground conditions were hard: summer heat in the lower levels, constant moisture, the acrid smell of blasting powder, and ever-present silica dust that shortened miners' lives and left many in later years disabled by silicosis. An unusually rich ore chimney was discovered when development drifts on two adjacent levels connected unexpectedly through the ore body itself, revealing a vertical pipe of native metal that astonished even veterans. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. Total recorded production reached $149,697 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The ruins of the mill foundation, the flat ribbon of gray tailings, and the gated collar of the main shaft are all that remain of a property that once employed hundreds. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.

mines.name: Joe Dandy Mine
mines.description: In the second year of the big rush, 1910, the Joe Dandy Mine differentiated itself from dozens of flash-in-the-pan ventures by hitting a thick, continuous ore body at only forty feet of depth. High-grade pockets produced spectacular specimens — wire gold, leaf gold, and crystalline masses — some of which were sold directly to collectors at a premium over bullion value. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. Labor relations were tense through much of the mine's life; the Western Federation of Miners organized the workforce in the early twentieth century, and a series of strikes tested management's resolve. A winter avalanche destroyed the lower section of the aerial tramway and isolated the mine from its mill for seven weeks; the company used the forced downtime to rehabilitate the underground ventilation system. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. The mine's cumulative output of $69,514 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. County historical records and a collection of mine logbooks preserved at the state historical society give researchers unusually detailed information about this property's operational history. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.

mines.name: Junior Mine
mines.description: Backed by modest Eastern capital and big local ambition, the Junior Mine sank its first shaft in 1916 and quickly proved that the quartz veins of this district ran deeper and richer than surface sampling had suggested. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. Miners were paid by the contract system in the early years, each man responsible for a set amount of advance per shift; the system rewarded skill and speed but created fierce competition and occasional shortcuts on safety. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Production records show $458,938 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.

mines.name: Kathleen Mine
mines.description: The Kathleen Mine was incorporated in 1912 on ground that Indian and Mexican miners had worked by hand for years; the new company brought steam power and ambition to what had been a subsistence operation. High-grade pockets produced spectacular specimens — wire gold, leaf gold, and crystalline masses — some of which were sold directly to collectors at a premium over bullion value. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. The mine's fatality rate was higher than average in its early years, reflecting both the dangerous ore body and management's tendency to prioritize production over safety improvements. The ore shoots plunged steeply to the northeast, meaning that the richest ground was always at the deepest accessible level — a geological reality that drove ever-deeper shaft sinking. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. The mine's cumulative output of $284,653 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. County historical records and a collection of mine logbooks preserved at the state historical society give researchers unusually detailed information about this property's operational history. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.

mines.name: Lida Mine
mines.description: Winter did not stop the founding of the Lida Mine; in 1905, men working frozen ground with picks and bars broke into a gold-bearing structure that would define the next two decades. The principal ore mineral was free-milling gold locked in quartz, simple enough to concentrate in a stamp mill and amalgamate on copper plates without expensive chemical treatment. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The mine's cumulative output of $339,503 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The region's mining heritage tourism industry has found the Lida Mine site to be one of its most evocative stops, combining accessible ruins with a well-documented historical record. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.

mines.description: The Lone Star Mine dates to 1891, a period when the rush was still fresh and optimism ran high enough to justify the heavy capitalization needed to build a proper stamp mill. The mine's Chinese workers, hired for surface and timbering tasks at wages below those of white miners, formed a self-contained community at the edge of camp and maintained ties to a Chinese-operated store in the nearest town. The vein system was part of a larger epithermal gold-silver network that geologists now understand as a single hydrothermal cell; at the time, miners just followed the ore wherever it led. mines.name: Lucky Bell Mine
mines.description: In the second year of the big rush, 1906, the Lucky Bell Mine differentiated itself from dozens of flash-in-the-pan ventures by hitting a thick, continuous ore body at only forty feet of depth. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. Wages at this mine tracked the district average closely, and the company avoided some of the worst labor conflicts of the era by maintaining steady employment through the inevitable ore-grade fluctuations. A granite porphyry intrusion had baked the surrounding sedimentary rocks into a resistant hornfels, and the skarn contact between the two formations proved to be the most productive ore zone in the district. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Total recorded production reached $431,284 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.

mines.name: Majestic Mine
mines.description: Steam drills and organized capital defined the Majestic Mine from its founding in 1904, when the original promoters vowed to work the deposit at a scale that hand-labor could never achieve. Free gold could be seen with the naked eye in the richest stopes, and careful mucking by experienced miners salvaged small pockets that would have been lost in a less attentive operation. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. Labor relations were tense through much of the mine's life; the Western Federation of Miners organized the workforce in the early twentieth century, and a series of strikes tested management's resolve. A cave of the hanging wall at the deepest level sent shock waves through the entire underground workings and brought men scrambling from every portal, but miraculously caused no fatalities. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. By final closure the mine had yielded $358,744 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. mines.name: Midget Mine
mines.description: A chance glint of free gold in the tailings of an abandoned hydraulic pit led to the founding of the Midget Mine in 1906, launching what would become one of the district's longer-lived operations. Assay values in the upper levels ran surprisingly rich in free gold, allowing the operators to ship direct to the smelter without on-site milling during the first years. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. Immigrant miners from Bohemia, Scotland, Wales, and Cornwall brought specific craft knowledge — the Welsh for their understanding of timbering, the Cornish for deep-shaft methods — that shaped the mine's technical character. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. By final closure the mine had yielded $370,581 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings.
