Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. The mine's Chinese workers, hired for surface and timbering tasks at wages below those of white miners, formed a self-contained community at the edge of camp and maintained ties to a Chinese-operated store in the nearest town. A winter avalanche destroyed the lower section of the aerial tramway and isolated the mine from its mill for seven weeks; the company used the forced downtime to rehabilitate the underground ventilation system. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. The mine's cumulative output of $194,950 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Mountain Slide Mine
mines.description: In the second year of the big rush, 1895, the Mountain Slide Mine differentiated itself from dozens of flash-in-the-pan ventures by hitting a thick, continuous ore body at only forty feet of depth. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. The nearby town that developed to service the mine's workforce was a rough-edged but functional community, its main street lined with frame buildings that housed every service a working miner's paycheck could buy. Two parallel veins converged at depth into a single rich structure, a geological geometry that the mine's resident engineer had predicted on theoretical grounds but proved only after years of systematic development. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. Production records show $79,557 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The property is now managed as a recreational area; the underground workings are permanently sealed, but surface roads, tailings, and foundation outlines are accessible to visitors. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: New York Mine
mines.description: The New York Mine was among the first deep mines in the district, sinking past the 1899 surface placers to reach the primary lode that would prove its real value. The oxidized zone proved exceptionally rich, but the transition to primary sulfide ore at the water table required the company to redesign its milling circuit at significant cost. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. Underground conditions were hard: summer heat in the lower levels, constant moisture, the acrid smell of blasting powder, and ever-present silica dust that shortened miners' lives and left many in later years disabled by silicosis. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Total recorded production reached $330,943 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Night Hawk Mine
mines.description: The Night Hawk Mine traces its origins to 1909, when a prospector whose horse stumbled on the hillside noticed the exposed quartz float and recognized the classic markers of a primary gold deposit. Assay values in the upper levels ran surprisingly rich in free gold, allowing the operators to ship direct to the smelter without on-site milling during the first years. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. Underground conditions were hard: summer heat in the lower levels, constant moisture, the acrid smell of blasting powder, and ever-present silica dust that shortened miners' lives and left many in later years disabled by silicosis. The deposit was zoned: near-surface gold grading into silver-dominant ore at depth, with lead-zinc values increasing in the lowest levels — a telescoped epithermal system that geologists found instructive. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. By final closure the mine had yielded $296,760 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Ophir Mine
mines.description: The Ophir Mine was staked in 1901 by a consortium of seasoned drifters who pooled their last funds to sink a test shaft — and struck the vein that justified every cent. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. The company maintained a library of technical and general reading for its employees — an early example of the paternalistic amenities that larger mining corporations were beginning to offer in the reform era. An unusually rich ore chimney was discovered when development drifts on two adjacent levels connected unexpectedly through the ore body itself, revealing a vertical pipe of native metal that astonished even veterans. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The mine's cumulative output of $221,407 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The property is now managed as a recreational area; the underground workings are permanently sealed, but surface roads, tailings, and foundation outlines are accessible to visitors. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Pauper Mine
mines.description: A chance glint of free gold in the tailings of an abandoned hydraulic pit led to the founding of the Pauper Mine in 1910, launching what would become one of the district's longer-lived operations. Assay values in the upper levels ran surprisingly rich in free gold, allowing the operators to ship direct to the smelter without on-site milling during the first years. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. The mine employed a physician during peak operations, a relatively unusual luxury that spoke to the company's interest in keeping its skilled workforce healthy and on-site. A cave-in on the fourth level trapped fourteen men for thirty-six hours before rescuers broke through from an adjacent drift, an event that prompted the company to adopt a more rigorous ground-support standard. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. The mine's cumulative output of $288,751 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The site draws rockhounds and history buffs; the tailings still yield fragments of the original ore, and the preserved headframe serves as a landmark visible for miles. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Phenix Mine
mines.description: Gold fever brought Phenix Mine to life in 1905, when prospectors following a free-gold float trace up a creek drainage broke into a promising quartz vein and staked the first claims. Assay values in the upper levels ran surprisingly rich in free gold, allowing the operators to ship direct to the smelter without on-site milling during the first years. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. The workforce was drawn from across the mining West: Cornish 'Cousin Jacks' ran the drilling crews, Irish muckers kept the stopes clean, Scandinavians handled the timber work, and Chinese laborers managed the most dangerous surface jobs. A granite porphyry intrusion had baked the surrounding sedimentary rocks into a resistant hornfels, and the skarn contact between the two formations proved to be the most productive ore zone in the district. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. Total recorded production reached $277,498 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and rusting machinery form an open-air industrial museum that attracts photographers and history enthusiasts. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Plymouth Rock Mine
mines.description: When prospectors traced a gold-bearing quartz reef up the canyon wall in 1904, the Plymouth Rock Mine came into existence almost overnight, drawing capital and labor from across the territory. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. Veterans of the Comstock, the California camps, and the Colorado silver fields found their way here, contributing deep practical knowledge to a management that was often long on capital and short on mining experience. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. Scale was limited by the narrow width of the ore body; the mine could not employ the large crews and heavy equipment that dominated the district's major operations. The mine's cumulative output of $94,630 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Poverty Mine
mines.description: The Poverty Mine began as a placer strike in 1913: a forty-niner scooping creek gravel noticed colors rich enough to warrant hard-rock follow-up, and within months a shaft was sinking. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. The mine's Chinese workers, hired for surface and timbering tasks at wages below those of white miners, formed a self-contained community at the edge of camp and maintained ties to a Chinese-operated store in the nearest town. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. The mine's cumulative output of $101,486 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The site is accessible via a graded dirt road maintained by the county, and the surviving structures are visible from the road without the need to enter the fenced hazard zone around the shaft collar. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Prince Albert Mine
mines.description: The Prince Albert Mine was organized in 1911 after the district's assayer confirmed gold values that made even the most skeptical investors reach for their checkbooks. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. Tent cities gave way to frame structures within the first year, and within three years the camp had taken on the permanence of a real town, complete with a debating society and a brass band. Country rock alterations — pervasive silicification, adularia, and bladed calcite — gave the underground workings a distinctive appearance and confirmed the high-temperature hydrothermal origin of the ore. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Production records show $342,912 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Years of weathering and vandalism have reduced the surface structures to their essence, but the mine remains a recognizable industrial landscape rather than just a scar on the hillside. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.

mines.name: Red Bird Mine
mines.description: When the placer gold played out upstream in 1915, savvy prospectors followed the eroded channel to its source and established the Red Bird Mine on the bedrock reef. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. The mine employed a physician during peak operations, a relatively unusual luxury that spoke to the company's interest in keeping its skilled workforce healthy and on-site. A catastrophic underground fire in the upper stopes destroyed several hundred feet of timber sets and forced a three-month shutdown while the burned-out sections were rehabilitated. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The mine's cumulative output of $298,101 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Years of weathering and vandalism have reduced the surface structures to their essence, but the mine remains a recognizable industrial landscape rather than just a scar on the hillside. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.

mines.name: Rexford Mine
mines.description: In 1918, a surveying crew camping on the ridge noticed yellow flecks in their fire ring stones, and the Rexford Mine was born from that accidental discovery. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. The mine's Chinese workers, hired for surface and timbering tasks at wages below those of white miners, formed a self-contained community at the edge of camp and maintained ties to a Chinese-operated store in the nearest town. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. Total recorded production reached $146,760 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.

mines.name: Rochester Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1905 by a stock company formed in the state capital, the Rochester Mine quickly justified its investors' faith when the first deep level revealed ore far richer than the near-surface sampling had suggested. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. Women's contributions to the camp economy were substantial and often overlooked; they ran boarding houses, laundries, and small stores that collectively processed more money than the mine itself in some lean years. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. The mine's cumulative output of $449,641 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Years of weathering and vandalism have reduced the surface structures to their essence, but the mine remains a recognizable industrial landscape rather than just a scar on the hillside. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.

mines.name: Rock Foundry Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1904 on a hill already pocked with the shallow pits of earlier searchers, the Rock Foundry Mine went deeper and richer than any of its predecessors. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. The mine's fatality rate was higher than average in its early years, reflecting both the dangerous ore body and management's tendency to prioritize production over safety improvements. The ore shoots plunged steeply to the northeast, meaning that the richest ground was always at the deepest accessible level — a geological reality that drove ever-deeper shaft sinking. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The mine's cumulative output of $202,519 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The site draws rockhounds and history buffs; the tailings still yield fragments of the original ore, and the preserved headframe serves as a landmark visible for miles. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.

mines.name: Scarlett Mine
mines.description: The Scarlett Mine was organized in 1905 after the district's assayer confirmed gold values that made even the most skeptical investors reach for their checkbooks. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. Labor relations were tense through much of the mine's life; the Western Federation of Miners organized the workforce in the early twentieth century, and a series of strikes tested management's resolve. A spectacular ore pocket discovered on the seventh level — massive, almost pure native metal — funded a complete renovation of the mill and hoist plant that set the mine up for another decade of profitable operation. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The mine's cumulative output of $229,429 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Preservation efforts have protected the most significant surface structures; the mine is listed on state and national historic registers and continues to attract researchers studying the industrial archaeology of western mining. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.

mines.name: Silver Dollar Mine
mines.description: The Silver Dollar Mine was staked in 1914 by a consortium of seasoned drifters who pooled their last funds to sink a test shaft — and struck the vein that justified every cent. High-grade pockets produced spectacular specimens — wire gold, leaf gold, and crystalline masses — some of which were sold directly to collectors at a premium over bullion value. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. Wages at this mine tracked the district average closely, and the company avoided some of the worst labor conflicts of the era by maintaining steady employment through the inevitable ore-grade fluctuations. Silicification of the wallrock created a hard, persistent hanging wall that minimized dilution and reduced the need for extensive timbering in the upper levels. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. Total recorded production reached $92,789 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. A local historical society has stabilized the surviving headframe and ore bins, and occasional guided tours bring a trickle of visitors who connect the industrial ruin to the period photographs on display inside. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.

mines.name: Snowbird Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1904 by a stock company formed in the state capital, the Snowbird Mine quickly justified its investors' faith when the first deep level revealed ore far richer than the near-surface sampling had suggested. The principal ore mineral was free-milling gold locked in quartz, simple enough to concentrate in a stamp mill and amalgamate on copper plates without expensive chemical treatment. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. A mutual aid society formed by the miners provided modest death benefits and sick pay, filling a welfare gap that the company itself was slow to address. The discovery of a second ore body, separated from the main vein by a barren porphyry dike, was made by an experienced shifter who noticed a change in wall-rock alteration that others had dismissed. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. The mine's cumulative output of $190,636 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Decades after final closure, the mine's footprint — shafts, adits, tailings, mill site — remains one of the most prominent physical features in this part of the district. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.

mines.description: Gold fever brought Spring Creek Mine to life in 1899, when prospectors following a free-gold float trace up a creek drainage broke into a promising quartz vein and staked the first claims. mines.name: Summit Mine
mines.description: Brothers who had worked the Comstock bonanzas for a decade brought their savings and their skills to this district in 1906 and founded the Summit Mine on a vein they traced across three separate outcroppings. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. Family men who planned to stay rather than drift formed the backbone of the camp's permanent population, building small farms on nearby land and sending their children to the district school. During a record snowfall winter, supply wagons could not reach the mine for six weeks, and the crew worked on reduced rations until the road was reopened — a testament to the determination of the men and their families. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. Production records show $80,210 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The camp once bustled with the noise of stamps, drills, and hoists; today it is silent except for the wind, the old camp roads slowly disappearing under encroaching vegetation. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.

mines.name: Tidal Wave Mine
mines.description: The Tidal Wave Mine was incorporated in 1917 on ground that Indian and Mexican miners had worked by hand for years; the new company brought steam power and ambition to what had been a subsistence operation. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. The ore shoots plunged steeply to the northeast, meaning that the richest ground was always at the deepest accessible level — a geological reality that drove ever-deeper shaft sinking. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. The mine's cumulative output of $88,414 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Little remains above ground: the concrete foundation of the shaft house, the long gray ribbon of tailings, and a few ore-bin timbers slowly returning to the soil. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Triangle Mine
mines.description: In 1904, a surveying crew camping on the ridge noticed yellow flecks in their fire ring stones, and the Triangle Mine was born from that accidental discovery. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. Labor relations were tense through much of the mine's life; the Western Federation of Miners organized the workforce in the early twentieth century, and a series of strikes tested management's resolve. A labor walkout in the mine's peak year idled the property for nearly three months, during which the owners brought in outside contractors who struggled to maintain the complex underground water balance. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. By final closure the mine had yielded $93,454 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The property is now managed as a recreational area; the underground workings are permanently sealed, but surface roads, tailings, and foundation outlines are accessible to visitors. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Two Bears Mine
mines.description: The Two Bears Mine was incorporated in 1914 following a season of intensive surface sampling that convinced its organizers the deposit was large enough to justify a major capital investment. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. A two-room schoolhouse built by the mining company's wives' auxiliary educated the district's children through all twelve grades at its peak enrollment. The ore body's dip carried it under a neighboring claim, creating the legal disputes over apex rights that were common in this era and that eventually required a settlement negotiated by outside attorneys. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. By final closure the mine had yielded $217,691 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Years of weathering and vandalism have reduced the surface structures to their essence, but the mine remains a recognizable industrial landscape rather than just a scar on the hillside. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Union Mine
mines.description: Winter did not stop the founding of the Union Mine; in 1895, men working frozen ground with picks and bars broke into a gold-bearing structure that would define the next two decades. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. The mine's payroll was the economic engine of the surrounding county, supporting farms, freighters, and merchants who collectively formed a community larger than the mine alone could explain. Smallpox swept the camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and forcing the temporary suspension of production until the epidemic passed and the workforce could safely reassemble. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Total recorded production reached $300,367 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. Years of weathering and vandalism have reduced the surface structures to their essence, but the mine remains a recognizable industrial landscape rather than just a scar on the hillside. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Ute Chief Mine
mines.description: An experienced drift miner who had spent fifteen years in the California camps identified the ore structure at this site in 1909 and organized the Ute Chief Mine with a handful of trusted partners. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. The mine's machine shop could fabricate most replacement parts on-site, reducing the costly delays that plagued more remote operations dependent on long supply chains. Labor relations were tense through much of the mine's life; the Western Federation of Miners organized the workforce in the early twentieth century, and a series of strikes tested management's resolve. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Production records show $480,481 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The site draws rockhounds and history buffs; the tailings still yield fragments of the original ore, and the preserved headframe serves as a landmark visible for miles. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Vanderbilt Mine
mines.description: A government geologist mapping the district in 1908 noted the gold-bearing structure in his survey report; within months, private capital had organized the Vanderbilt Mine and begun sinking. The footwall side of the vein consistently ran higher in gold than the hanging wall, and experienced miners learned to work the ore selectively to maximize recovery and minimize dilution. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. The post office, which served the camp and surrounding district for three decades, was the connection to the wider world; its records give historians one of their most reliable windows into the composition of the workforce. A runaway ore train on the inclined haulage wrecked the ore bin and damaged the crusher, shutting down production for three weeks while repairs were made and the haulage brake system redesigned. The mine was a modest operation by district standards, employing rarely more than twenty-five men and running a small stamp mill that processed ore in batches rather than continuously. By final closure the mine had yielded $198,341 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Aerial photographs reveal the full extent of the underground workings through surface subsidence patterns — shallow depressions that trace the main vein system for nearly half a mile across the hillside. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Victor Gold Mine
mines.description: Winter did not stop the founding of the Victor Gold Mine; in 1894, men working frozen ground with picks and bars broke into a gold-bearing structure that would define the next two decades. The oxidized zone proved exceptionally rich, but the transition to primary sulfide ore at the water table required the company to redesign its milling circuit at significant cost. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. A small town grew up around the mine: a post office, a two-room schoolhouse, three saloons, and the inevitable assay office that doubled as banker and source of local credit. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The mine's cumulative output of $234,864 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The site is accessible via a graded dirt road maintained by the county, and the surviving structures are visible from the road without the need to enter the fenced hazard zone around the shaft collar. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Waverley Mine
mines.description: A chance glint of free gold in the tailings of an abandoned hydraulic pit led to the founding of the Waverley Mine in 1918, launching what would become one of the district's longer-lived operations. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. A polyglot workforce gave the camp its distinctive character: Serbian and Croatian miners who had come from the copper mines of Central Europe occupied one end of camp, while Mexican miners who had followed silver veins north formed a tight-knit community at the other. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. Production records show $349,403 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The stone assay office walls remain standing — thick enough to withstand a century of weather and neglect — while the surrounding wooden structures have long since collapsed. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Whittier Mine
mines.description: The Whittier Mine was among the first deep mines in the district, sinking past the 1915 surface placers to reach the primary lode that would prove its real value. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. The workforce was drawn from across the mining West: Cornish 'Cousin Jacks' ran the drilling crews, Irish muckers kept the stopes clean, Scandinavians handled the timber work, and Chinese laborers managed the most dangerous surface jobs. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Production records show $240,322 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Aerial photographs reveal the full extent of the underground workings through surface subsidence patterns — shallow depressions that trace the main vein system for nearly half a mile across the hillside. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Wild Horse Mine
mines.description: The Wild Horse Mine opened in 1914 after a grizzled veteran spotted the telltale iron-stained gossan capping a rich lode — the kind of surface sign that experienced eyes never miss. The footwall side of the vein consistently ran higher in gold than the hanging wall, and experienced miners learned to work the ore selectively to maximize recovery and minimize dilution. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. The company maintained a library of technical and general reading for its employees — an early example of the paternalistic amenities that larger mining corporations were beginning to offer in the reform era. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. By final closure the mine had yielded $73,151 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Surface structures were demolished for salvage within a decade of closure, leaving only the tailings, a few stone walls, and the collapsed collar of the main shaft to mark the location. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Chrysolite Mine
mines.description: Winter did not stop the founding of the Chrysolite Mine; in 1895, men working frozen ground with picks and bars broke into a gold-bearing structure that would define the next two decades. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. The mine's payroll was the economic engine of the surrounding county, supporting farms, freighters, and merchants who collectively formed a community larger than the mine alone could explain. The ore body was controlled by a northwest-trending fault that focused hydrothermal fluids against an impermeable diorite dike — classic structural geology that the mine's long-time geologist had predicted before the drill proved it. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The mine's cumulative output of $416,230 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Decades after final closure, the mine's footprint — shafts, adits, tailings, mill site — remains one of the most prominent physical features in this part of the district. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Robert E. Lee Mine
mines.description: The Robert E. Lee Mine was incorporated in 1899 following a season of intensive surface sampling that convinced its organizers the deposit was large enough to justify a major capital investment. The oxidized zone proved exceptionally rich, but the transition to primary sulfide ore at the water table required the company to redesign its milling circuit at significant cost. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. The mine superintendent's house — larger and better furnished than the miners' cabins — stood on a slight rise above the camp, visible evidence of the social hierarchy that organized mining communities. A legal dispute over apex rights with a neighboring claim lasted four years in the courts and consumed legal fees that might otherwise have funded two additional levels of development. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. Production records show $138,016 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Little remains above ground: the concrete foundation of the shaft house, the long gray ribbon of tailings, and a few ore-bin timbers slowly returning to the soil. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Little Pittsburgh Mine
mines.description: When prospectors traced a gold-bearing quartz reef up the canyon wall in 1909, the Little Pittsburgh Mine came into existence almost overnight, drawing capital and labor from across the territory. Assay values in the upper levels ran surprisingly rich in free gold, allowing the operators to ship direct to the smelter without on-site milling during the first years. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. The mine employed a physician during peak operations, a relatively unusual luxury that spoke to the company's interest in keeping its skilled workforce healthy and on-site. A spectacular ore pocket discovered on the seventh level — massive, almost pure native metal — funded a complete renovation of the mill and hoist plant that set the mine up for another decade of profitable operation. Scale was limited by the narrow width of the ore body; the mine could not employ the large crews and heavy equipment that dominated the district's major operations. By final closure the mine had yielded $199,134 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The camp once bustled with the noise of stamps, drills, and hoists; today it is silent except for the wind, the old camp roads slowly disappearing under encroaching vegetation. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Carbonate Mine
mines.description: The Carbonate Mine was among the first deep mines in the district, sinking past the 1907 surface placers to reach the primary lode that would prove its real value. The oxidized zone proved exceptionally rich, but the transition to primary sulfide ore at the water table required the company to redesign its milling circuit at significant cost. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. The company maintained a library of technical and general reading for its employees — an early example of the paternalistic amenities that larger mining corporations were beginning to offer in the reform era. Smallpox swept the camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and forcing the temporary suspension of production until the epidemic passed and the workforce could safely reassemble. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. The mine's cumulative output of $205,577 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Iron Silver Mine
mines.description: Steam drills and organized capital defined the Iron Silver Mine from its founding in 1883, when the original promoters vowed to work the deposit at a scale that hand-labor could never achieve. The oxidized zone proved exceptionally rich, but the transition to primary sulfide ore at the water table required the company to redesign its milling circuit at significant cost. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. A polyglot workforce gave the camp its distinctive character: Serbian and Croatian miners who had come from the copper mines of Central Europe occupied one end of camp, while Mexican miners who had followed silver veins north formed a tight-knit community at the other. Country rock alterations — pervasive silicification, adularia, and bladed calcite — gave the underground workings a distinctive appearance and confirmed the high-temperature hydrothermal origin of the ore. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The mine's cumulative output of $276,064 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Environmental remediation has cleaned up the worst contamination from tailings runoff, but the site retains enough industrial character to clearly communicate its mining heritage. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.

mines.name: Morning Star Mine
mines.description: Gold fever brought Morning Star Mine to life in 1912, when prospectors following a free-gold float trace up a creek drainage broke into a promising quartz vein and staked the first claims. The footwall side of the vein consistently ran higher in gold than the hanging wall, and experienced miners learned to work the ore selectively to maximize recovery and minimize dilution. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. The mine's cumulative output of $285,834 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. mines.description: Brothers who had worked the Comstock bonanzas for a decade brought their savings and their skills to this district in 1894 and founded the Ibex Mine on a vein they traced across three separate outcroppings. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. mines.name: Adelaide Mine
mines.description: The Adelaide Mine dates to 1904, a period when the rush was still fresh and optimism ran high enough to justify the heavy capitalization needed to build a proper stamp mill. Telluride minerals — calaverite and sylvanite — appeared at depth alongside the free-gold ore, and their assay values sometimes exceeded the free-milling upper levels. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. A winter avalanche destroyed the lower section of the aerial tramway and isolated the mine from its mill for seven weeks; the company used the forced downtime to rehabilitate the underground ventilation system. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Production records show $553,287 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. A state historic marker at the road junction explains the mine's significance; visitors who venture up the old access road find the ruins more extensive than the marker suggests. mines.name: Agassiz Mine
mines.description: Backed by modest Eastern capital and big local ambition, the Agassiz Mine sank its first shaft in 1912 and quickly proved that the quartz veins of this district ran deeper and richer than surface sampling had suggested. The principal ore mineral was free-milling gold locked in quartz, simple enough to concentrate in a stamp mill and amalgamate on copper plates without expensive chemical treatment. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. Labor relations were tense through much of the mine's life; the Western Federation of Miners organized the workforce in the early twentieth century, and a series of strikes tested management's resolve. The ore shoots plunged steeply to the northeast, meaning that the richest ground was always at the deepest accessible level — a geological reality that drove ever-deeper shaft sinking. Total recorded production reached $94,702 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers.
