Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. The mine's payroll was the economic engine of the surrounding county, supporting farms, freighters, and merchants who collectively formed a community larger than the mine alone could explain. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. By final closure the mine had yielded $249,968 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Aerial photographs reveal the full extent of the underground workings through surface subsidence patterns — shallow depressions that trace the main vein system for nearly half a mile across the hillside. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Nellie Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1897 on a hill already pocked with the shallow pits of earlier searchers, the Nellie Mine went deeper and richer than any of its predecessors. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. A mutual aid society formed by the miners provided modest death benefits and sick pay, filling a welfare gap that the company itself was slow to address. The deposit was zoned: near-surface gold grading into silver-dominant ore at depth, with lead-zinc values increasing in the lowest levels — a telescoped epithermal system that geologists found instructive. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. By final closure the mine had yielded $417,986 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Pandora Mine
mines.description: When the placer gold played out upstream in 1892, savvy prospectors followed the eroded channel to its source and established the Pandora Mine on the bedrock reef. The principal ore mineral was free-milling gold locked in quartz, simple enough to concentrate in a stamp mill and amalgamate on copper plates without expensive chemical treatment. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. Family men who planned to stay rather than drift formed the backbone of the camp's permanent population, building small farms on nearby land and sending their children to the district school. A runaway ore train on the inclined haulage wrecked the ore bin and damaged the crusher, shutting down production for three weeks while repairs were made and the haulage brake system redesigned. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. By final closure the mine had yielded $219,873 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Alta Mine
mines.description: Gold fever brought Alta Mine to life in 1888, when prospectors following a free-gold float trace up a creek drainage broke into a promising quartz vein and staked the first claims. The principal ore mineral was free-milling gold locked in quartz, simple enough to concentrate in a stamp mill and amalgamate on copper plates without expensive chemical treatment. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. A small town grew up around the mine: a post office, a two-room schoolhouse, three saloons, and the inevitable assay office that doubled as banker and source of local credit. A legal dispute over apex rights with a neighboring claim lasted four years in the courts and consumed legal fees that might otherwise have funded two additional levels of development. The mine was a modest operation by district standards, employing rarely more than twenty-five men and running a small stamp mill that processed ore in batches rather than continuously. The mine's cumulative output of $96,662 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Little remains above ground: the concrete foundation of the shaft house, the long gray ribbon of tailings, and a few ore-bin timbers slowly returning to the soil. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Argentine Mine
mines.description: A government geologist mapping the district in 1885 noted the gold-bearing structure in his survey report; within months, private capital had organized the Argentine Mine and begun sinking. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. A small town grew up around the mine: a post office, a two-room schoolhouse, three saloons, and the inevitable assay office that doubled as banker and source of local credit. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. By final closure the mine had yielded $314,690 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Years of weathering and vandalism have reduced the surface structures to their essence, but the mine remains a recognizable industrial landscape rather than just a scar on the hillside. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Black Bear Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1890 by a stock company formed in the state capital, the Black Bear Mine quickly justified its investors' faith when the first deep level revealed ore far richer than the near-surface sampling had suggested. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. Wages at this mine tracked the district average closely, and the company avoided some of the worst labor conflicts of the era by maintaining steady employment through the inevitable ore-grade fluctuations. The vein system was part of a larger epithermal gold-silver network that geologists now understand as a single hydrothermal cell; at the time, miners just followed the ore wherever it led. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. By final closure the mine had yielded $494,843 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and rusting machinery form an open-air industrial museum that attracts photographers and history enthusiasts. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Butterfly-Terrible Mine
mines.description: A party of Welsh hard-rock miners who arrived in 1900 recognized the geology at once — the pyritized schist, the carbonate alteration — and staked the Butterfly-Terrible Mine before the week was out. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. Family men who planned to stay rather than drift formed the backbone of the camp's permanent population, building small farms on nearby land and sending their children to the district school. Flooding in the lower workings following an unusually wet spring required emergency pumping operations that ran for six weeks before the water level was brought back under control. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. By final closure the mine had yielded $228,528 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The stone assay office walls remain standing — thick enough to withstand a century of weather and neglect — while the surrounding wooden structures have long since collapsed. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Carbonero Mine
mines.description: A chance glint of free gold in the tailings of an abandoned hydraulic pit led to the founding of the Carbonero Mine in 1887, launching what would become one of the district's longer-lived operations. Telluride minerals — calaverite and sylvanite — appeared at depth alongside the free-gold ore, and their assay values sometimes exceeded the free-milling upper levels. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. Wages at this mine tracked the district average closely, and the company avoided some of the worst labor conflicts of the era by maintaining steady employment through the inevitable ore-grade fluctuations. During a record snowfall winter, supply wagons could not reach the mine for six weeks, and the crew worked on reduced rations until the road was reopened — a testament to the determination of the men and their families. The mine was a modest operation by district standards, employing rarely more than twenty-five men and running a small stamp mill that processed ore in batches rather than continuously. Production records show $173,409 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The camp once bustled with the noise of stamps, drills, and hoists; today it is silent except for the wind, the old camp roads slowly disappearing under encroaching vegetation. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Cimarron Mine
mines.description: In 1887, a surveying crew camping on the ridge noticed yellow flecks in their fire ring stones, and the Cimarron Mine was born from that accidental discovery. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. The nearby town that developed to service the mine's workforce was a rough-edged but functional community, its main street lined with frame buildings that housed every service a working miner's paycheck could buy. A cave-in on the fourth level trapped fourteen men for thirty-six hours before rescuers broke through from an adjacent drift, an event that prompted the company to adopt a more rigorous ground-support standard. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. Production records show $182,865 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Today the old mine yard is quiet, the headframe long since dismantled for salvage, but the outline of the ore bins and the concrete footings of the hoist room are still legible to a practiced eye. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Columbus Mine
mines.description: The Columbus Mine was among the first deep mines in the district, sinking past the 1889 surface placers to reach the primary lode that would prove its real value. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. The mine's Chinese workers, hired for surface and timbering tasks at wages below those of white miners, formed a self-contained community at the edge of camp and maintained ties to a Chinese-operated store in the nearest town. A stock promotion scandal in the mine's middle years drove out the original investors and brought in new management, which proved to be an inadvertent improvement: the new superintendent was a better practical miner than his predecessor. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Production records show $349,152 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Interpretive signs at the trailhead describe the mine's history for visitors exploring the surrounding public land; the actual mine site is a short walk from the parking area. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Confidence Mine
mines.description: The Confidence Mine dates to 1882, a period when the rush was still fresh and optimism ran high enough to justify the heavy capitalization needed to build a proper stamp mill. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. A mutual aid society formed by the miners provided modest death benefits and sick pay, filling a welfare gap that the company itself was slow to address. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. Scale was limited by the narrow width of the ore body; the mine could not employ the large crews and heavy equipment that dominated the district's major operations. Production records show $145,030 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The camp once bustled with the noise of stamps, drills, and hoists; today it is silent except for the wind, the old camp roads slowly disappearing under encroaching vegetation. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.

mines.name: Corn Stalk Mine
mines.description: The Corn Stalk Mine dates to 1909, a period when the rush was still fresh and optimism ran high enough to justify the heavy capitalization needed to build a proper stamp mill. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. The mine's payroll was the economic engine of the surrounding county, supporting farms, freighters, and merchants who collectively formed a community larger than the mine alone could explain. A stock promotion scandal in the mine's middle years drove out the original investors and brought in new management, which proved to be an inadvertent improvement: the new superintendent was a better practical miner than his predecessor. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The mine's cumulative output of $282,697 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The site is accessible via a graded dirt road maintained by the county, and the surviving structures are visible from the road without the need to enter the fenced hazard zone around the shaft collar. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.

mines.name: Esperanza Mine
mines.description: The Esperanza Mine dates to 1914, a period when the rush was still fresh and optimism ran high enough to justify the heavy capitalization needed to build a proper stamp mill. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. The company maintained a library of technical and general reading for its employees — an early example of the paternalistic amenities that larger mining corporations were beginning to offer in the reform era. The mine's machine shop could fabricate most replacement parts on-site, reducing the costly delays that plagued more remote operations dependent on long supply chains. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Total recorded production reached $424,741 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. Surface structures were demolished for salvage within a decade of closure, leaving only the tailings, a few stone walls, and the collapsed collar of the main shaft to mark the location. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.

mines.description: Cornish miners who had drifted north from the California camps staked the Gold King Mine in 1906, bringing their tradition of deep, systematic hard-rock work to what had been a surface-only district. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. The workforce was drawn from across the mining West: Cornish 'Cousin Jacks' ran the drilling crews, Irish muckers kept the stopes clean, Scandinavians handled the timber work, and Chinese laborers managed the most dangerous surface jobs. A legal dispute over apex rights with a neighboring claim lasted four years in the courts and consumed legal fees that might otherwise have funded two additional levels of development. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. Production records show $386,944 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and rusting machinery form an open-air industrial museum that attracts photographers and history enthusiasts. mines.name: Humboldt Mine
mines.description: A government geologist mapping the district in 1914 noted the gold-bearing structure in his survey report; within months, private capital had organized the Humboldt Mine and begun sinking. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. The mine's machine shop could fabricate most replacement parts on-site, reducing the costly delays that plagued more remote operations dependent on long supply chains. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. A small town grew up around the mine: a post office, a two-room schoolhouse, three saloons, and the inevitable assay office that doubled as banker and source of local credit. Country rock alterations — pervasive silicification, adularia, and bladed calcite — gave the underground workings a distinctive appearance and confirmed the high-temperature hydrothermal origin of the ore. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. mines.name: Japan Mine
mines.description: A government geologist mapping the district in 1898 noted the gold-bearing structure in his survey report; within months, private capital had organized the Japan Mine and begun sinking. High-grade pockets produced spectacular specimens — wire gold, leaf gold, and crystalline masses — some of which were sold directly to collectors at a premium over bullion value. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. Family men who planned to stay rather than drift formed the backbone of the camp's permanent population, building small farms on nearby land and sending their children to the district school. A spectacular ore pocket discovered on the seventh level — massive, almost pure native metal — funded a complete renovation of the mill and hoist plant that set the mine up for another decade of profitable operation. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. Production records show $474,838 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The site has been partially reclaimed; the tailings were graded and seeded, the shaft capped with concrete, and the most hazardous open workings fenced — though the outline of the camp is still unmistakable from above. mines.name: Kangaroo Mine
mines.description: Winter did not stop the founding of the Kangaroo Mine; in 1898, men working frozen ground with picks and bars broke into a gold-bearing structure that would define the next two decades. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. The mine's payroll was the economic engine of the surrounding county, supporting farms, freighters, and merchants who collectively formed a community larger than the mine alone could explain. The discovery of a second ore body, separated from the main vein by a barren porphyry dike, was made by an experienced shifter who noticed a change in wall-rock alteration that others had dismissed. Scale was limited by the narrow width of the ore body; the mine could not employ the large crews and heavy equipment that dominated the district's major operations. Total recorded production reached $132,460 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. Years of weathering and vandalism have reduced the surface structures to their essence, but the mine remains a recognizable industrial landscape rather than just a scar on the hillside. mines.name: Liberty Bell Mine
mines.description: An experienced drift miner who had spent fifteen years in the California camps identified the ore structure at this site in 1882 and organized the Liberty Bell Mine with a handful of trusted partners. The principal ore mineral was free-milling gold locked in quartz, simple enough to concentrate in a stamp mill and amalgamate on copper plates without expensive chemical treatment. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. The workforce was drawn from across the mining West: Cornish 'Cousin Jacks' ran the drilling crews, Irish muckers kept the stopes clean, Scandinavians handled the timber work, and Chinese laborers managed the most dangerous surface jobs. A catastrophic underground fire in the upper stopes destroyed several hundred feet of timber sets and forced a three-month shutdown while the burned-out sections were rehabilitated. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Production records show $285,082 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. County historical records and a collection of mine logbooks preserved at the state historical society give researchers unusually detailed information about this property's operational history. mines.name: Lone Tree Mine
mines.description: Cornish miners who had drifted north from the California camps staked the Lone Tree Mine in 1897, bringing their tradition of deep, systematic hard-rock work to what had been a surface-only district. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. A polyglot workforce gave the camp its distinctive character: Serbian and Croatian miners who had come from the copper mines of Central Europe occupied one end of camp, while Mexican miners who had followed silver veins north formed a tight-knit community at the other. The district's geology was more complex than the simple bonanza vein that early promoters had advertised; faults offset the ore body repeatedly, requiring careful surveying and systematic exploration to maintain continuity. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. Total recorded production reached $442,238 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The ruins of the mill foundation, the flat ribbon of gray tailings, and the gated collar of the main shaft are all that remain of a property that once employed hundreds. mines.name: Mendota Mine
mines.description: The Mendota Mine was staked in 1910 by a consortium of seasoned drifters who pooled their last funds to sink a test shaft — and struck the vein that justified every cent. High-grade pockets produced spectacular specimens — wire gold, leaf gold, and crystalline masses — some of which were sold directly to collectors at a premium over bullion value. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. Labor relations were tense through much of the mine's life; the Western Federation of Miners organized the workforce in the early twentieth century, and a series of strikes tested management's resolve. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. Production records show $95,612 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Environmental remediation has cleaned up the worst contamination from tailings runoff, but the site retains enough industrial character to clearly communicate its mining heritage. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.

mines.name: Montezuma Mine
mines.description: The Montezuma Mine was staked in 1907 by a consortium of seasoned drifters who pooled their last funds to sink a test shaft — and struck the vein that justified every cent. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. The mine's fatality rate was higher than average in its early years, reflecting both the dangerous ore body and management's tendency to prioritize production over safety improvements. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. Production records show $276,919 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Little remains above ground: the concrete foundation of the shaft house, the long gray ribbon of tailings, and a few ore-bin timbers slowly returning to the soil. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Nevada Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1881 on a hill already pocked with the shallow pits of earlier searchers, the Nevada Mine went deeper and richer than any of its predecessors. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. A polyglot workforce gave the camp its distinctive character: Serbian and Croatian miners who had come from the copper mines of Central Europe occupied one end of camp, while Mexican miners who had followed silver veins north formed a tight-knit community at the other. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. Production records show $236,994 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Newport Mine
mines.description: Winter did not stop the founding of the Newport Mine; in 1896, men working frozen ground with picks and bars broke into a gold-bearing structure that would define the next two decades. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. The mine's payroll was the economic engine of the surrounding county, supporting farms, freighters, and merchants who collectively formed a community larger than the mine alone could explain. A labor walkout in the mine's peak year idled the property for nearly three months, during which the owners brought in outside contractors who struggled to maintain the complex underground water balance. Scale was limited by the narrow width of the ore body; the mine could not employ the large crews and heavy equipment that dominated the district's major operations. Total recorded production reached $96,831 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The camp once bustled with the noise of stamps, drills, and hoists; today it is silent except for the wind, the old camp roads slowly disappearing under encroaching vegetation. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Ohio Mine
mines.description: A chance glint of free gold in the tailings of an abandoned hydraulic pit led to the founding of the Ohio Mine in 1883, launching what would become one of the district's longer-lived operations. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. The post office, which served the camp and surrounding district for three decades, was the connection to the wider world; its records give historians one of their most reliable windows into the composition of the workforce. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. By final closure the mine had yielded $355,204 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The stone assay office walls remain standing — thick enough to withstand a century of weather and neglect — while the surrounding wooden structures have long since collapsed. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Ottawa Mine
mines.description: Steam drills and organized capital defined the Ottawa Mine from its founding in 1910, when the original promoters vowed to work the deposit at a scale that hand-labor could never achieve. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. Immigrant miners from Bohemia, Scotland, Wales, and Cornwall brought specific craft knowledge — the Welsh for their understanding of timbering, the Cornish for deep-shaft methods — that shaped the mine's technical character. A granite porphyry intrusion had baked the surrounding sedimentary rocks into a resistant hornfels, and the skarn contact between the two formations proved to be the most productive ore zone in the district. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. By final closure the mine had yielded $244,552 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Philadelphia Mine
mines.description: A chance glint of free gold in the tailings of an abandoned hydraulic pit led to the founding of the Philadelphia Mine in 1915, launching what would become one of the district's longer-lived operations. Free gold could be seen with the naked eye in the richest stopes, and careful mucking by experienced miners salvaged small pockets that would have been lost in a less attentive operation. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. A union organizer arrived in the spring of the mine's busiest year and spent months carefully building a membership base before calling the strike that ultimately improved wage rates across the whole district. The ore body was controlled by a northwest-trending fault that focused hydrothermal fluids against an impermeable diorite dike — classic structural geology that the mine's long-time geologist had predicted before the drill proved it. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. By final closure the mine had yielded $326,072 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The region's mining heritage tourism industry has found the Philadelphia Mine site to be one of its most evocative stops, combining accessible ruins with a well-documented historical record. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Sherwood Mine
mines.description: When the placer gold played out upstream in 1880, savvy prospectors followed the eroded channel to its source and established the Sherwood Mine on the bedrock reef. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. The mine employed a physician during peak operations, a relatively unusual luxury that spoke to the company's interest in keeping its skilled workforce healthy and on-site. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. By final closure the mine had yielded $275,872 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Decades after final closure, the mine's footprint — shafts, adits, tailings, mill site — remains one of the most prominent physical features in this part of the district. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Sunnyside Mine
mines.description: An experienced drift miner who had spent fifteen years in the California camps identified the ore structure at this site in 1911 and organized the Sunnyside Mine with a handful of trusted partners. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. The mine employed a physician during peak operations, a relatively unusual luxury that spoke to the company's interest in keeping its skilled workforce healthy and on-site. A labor walkout in the mine's peak year idled the property for nearly three months, during which the owners brought in outside contractors who struggled to maintain the complex underground water balance. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. By final closure the mine had yielded $389,400 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The ruins of the mill foundation, the flat ribbon of gray tailings, and the gated collar of the main shaft are all that remain of a property that once employed hundreds. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Iowa Mine
mines.description: Gold fever brought Iowa Mine to life in 1895, when prospectors following a free-gold float trace up a creek drainage broke into a promising quartz vein and staked the first claims. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. A two-room schoolhouse built by the mining company's wives' auxiliary educated the district's children through all twelve grades at its peak enrollment. A stock promotion scandal in the mine's middle years drove out the original investors and brought in new management, which proved to be an inadvertent improvement: the new superintendent was a better practical miner than his predecessor. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. By final closure the mine had yielded $366,453 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The region's mining heritage tourism industry has found the Iowa Mine site to be one of its most evocative stops, combining accessible ruins with a well-documented historical record. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Old Hundred Mine
mines.description: The Old Hundred Mine was incorporated in 1907 on ground that Indian and Mexican miners had worked by hand for years; the new company brought steam power and ambition to what had been a subsistence operation. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. Labor relations were tense through much of the mine's life; the Western Federation of Miners organized the workforce in the early twentieth century, and a series of strikes tested management's resolve. A legal dispute over apex rights with a neighboring claim lasted four years in the courts and consumed legal fees that might otherwise have funded two additional levels of development. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. Production records show $143,922 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Interpretive signs at the trailhead describe the mine's history for visitors exploring the surrounding public land; the actual mine site is a short walk from the parking area. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Pride of the West Mine
mines.description: The Pride of the West Mine was organized in 1878 after the district's assayer confirmed gold values that made even the most skeptical investors reach for their checkbooks. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. The company maintained a library of technical and general reading for its employees — an early example of the paternalistic amenities that larger mining corporations were beginning to offer in the reform era. A cave-in on the fourth level trapped fourteen men for thirty-six hours before rescuers broke through from an adjacent drift, an event that prompted the company to adopt a more rigorous ground-support standard. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Production records show $290,756 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The site draws rockhounds and history buffs; the tailings still yield fragments of the original ore, and the preserved headframe serves as a landmark visible for miles. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Silver Lake Mine
mines.description: Backed by modest Eastern capital and big local ambition, the Silver Lake Mine sank its first shaft in 1896 and quickly proved that the quartz veins of this district ran deeper and richer than surface sampling had suggested. High-grade pockets produced spectacular specimens — wire gold, leaf gold, and crystalline masses — some of which were sold directly to collectors at a premium over bullion value. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. The mine's machine shop could fabricate most replacement parts on-site, reducing the costly delays that plagued more remote operations dependent on long supply chains. The mine superintendent's house — larger and better furnished than the miners' cabins — stood on a slight rise above the camp, visible evidence of the social hierarchy that organized mining communities. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Total recorded production reached $551,369 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. Aerial photographs reveal the full extent of the underground workings through surface subsidence patterns — shallow depressions that trace the main vein system for nearly half a mile across the hillside. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Mayflower Mine
mines.description: A party of Welsh hard-rock miners who arrived in 1880 recognized the geology at once — the pyritized schist, the carbonate alteration — and staked the Mayflower Mine before the week was out. Assay values in the upper levels ran surprisingly rich in free gold, allowing the operators to ship direct to the smelter without on-site milling during the first years. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. Immigrant miners from Bohemia, Scotland, Wales, and Cornwall brought specific craft knowledge — the Welsh for their understanding of timbering, the Cornish for deep-shaft methods — that shaped the mine's technical character. The district's geology was more complex than the simple bonanza vein that early promoters had advertised; faults offset the ore body repeatedly, requiring careful surveying and systematic exploration to maintain continuity. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Production records show $317,229 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Aspen Mine
mines.description: When the placer gold played out upstream in 1886, savvy prospectors followed the eroded channel to its source and established the Aspen Mine on the bedrock reef. The principal ore mineral was free-milling gold locked in quartz, simple enough to concentrate in a stamp mill and amalgamate on copper plates without expensive chemical treatment. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. The nearby town that developed to service the mine's workforce was a rough-edged but functional community, its main street lined with frame buildings that housed every service a working miner's paycheck could buy. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. Production records show $89,180 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Belcher Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1903 by a stock company formed in the state capital, the Belcher Mine quickly justified its investors' faith when the first deep level revealed ore far richer than the near-surface sampling had suggested. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. The mine's machine shop could fabricate most replacement parts on-site, reducing the costly delays that plagued more remote operations dependent on long supply chains. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. The mine's fatality rate was higher than average in its early years, reflecting both the dangerous ore body and management's tendency to prioritize production over safety improvements. A winter avalanche destroyed the lower section of the aerial tramway and isolated the mine from its mill for seven weeks; the company used the forced downtime to rehabilitate the underground ventilation system. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Production records show $555,583 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The site draws rockhounds and history buffs; the tailings still yield fragments of the original ore, and the preserved headframe serves as a landmark visible for miles. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.

mines.name: Black Dwarf Mine
mines.description: A government geologist mapping the district in 1908 noted the gold-bearing structure in his survey report; within months, private capital had organized the Black Dwarf Mine and begun sinking. Free gold could be seen with the naked eye in the richest stopes, and careful mucking by experienced miners salvaged small pockets that would have been lost in a less attentive operation. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. Miners were paid by the contract system in the early years, each man responsible for a set amount of advance per shift; the system rewarded skill and speed but created fierce competition and occasional shortcuts on safety. The vein system was part of a larger epithermal gold-silver network that geologists now understand as a single hydrothermal cell; at the time, miners just followed the ore wherever it led. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons.
