mines.name: Perigo Mine
mines.description: The Perigo Mine was staked in 1910 after chloride silver ore — rich, brittle, and unmistakable — was found cropping out of a gully wall that spring floods had freshly cut. Silver values were intimately associated with galena, and the concentrates commanded a favorable smelter schedule that rewarded careful ore preparation. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. Silicification of the wallrock created a hard, persistent hanging wall that minimized dilution and reduced the need for extensive timbering in the upper levels. Scale was limited by the narrow width of the ore body; the mine could not employ the large crews and heavy equipment that dominated the district's major operations. Total recorded production reached $138,839 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. A local historical society has stabilized the surviving headframe and ore bins, and occasional guided tours bring a trickle of visitors who connect the industrial ruin to the period photographs on display inside. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Prospect Mine
mines.description: A district already buzzing with silver excitement welcomed the Prospect Mine in 1875; the new property quickly proved that the ore body extended well beyond the surface outcrop. Ruby silver minerals — pyrargyrite and proustite — appeared in the richest ore shoots and were sometimes preserved as specimens rather than sent to the smelter. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. A union organizer arrived in the spring of the mine's busiest year and spent months carefully building a membership base before calling the strike that ultimately improved wage rates across the whole district. A catastrophic underground fire in the upper stopes destroyed several hundred feet of timber sets and forced a three-month shutdown while the burned-out sections were rehabilitated. Scale was limited by the narrow width of the ore body; the mine could not employ the large crews and heavy equipment that dominated the district's major operations. Total recorded production reached $39,392 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The surface plant has mostly vanished, but the mine's legacy persists in county records, family histories, and the occasional specimen jar of ore that turns up at a local antique fair. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Snowdrift Mine
mines.description: The Snowdrift Mine was founded in 1889 on the strength of assay returns that read like fiction — bonanza-grade silver in a thick carbonate vein that crossed the entire claim. Assay grades were erratic, reflecting the pocket nature of silver-bearing ore in this district; a week of bonanza followed a month of development work on barren vein matter. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. Tent cities gave way to frame structures within the first year, and within three years the camp had taken on the permanence of a real town, complete with a debating society and a brass band. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. Total recorded production reached $187,341 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Star of the West Mine
mines.description: A traveling assayer's offhand remark about the silver content of rocks near a mountain spring set off a staking rush in 1894 that placed the Star of the West Mine at its center. The silver occurred chiefly as argentite and native silver in a carbonate gangue, making hand-sorting practical and giving high-grade ore shipments an attractive economic profile. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. The mine's fatality rate was higher than average in its early years, reflecting both the dangerous ore body and management's tendency to prioritize production over safety improvements. Country rock alterations — pervasive silicification, adularia, and bladed calcite — gave the underground workings a distinctive appearance and confirmed the high-temperature hydrothermal origin of the ore. The mine was a modest operation by district standards, employing rarely more than twenty-five men and running a small stamp mill that processed ore in batches rather than continuously. Total recorded production reached $198,173 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The property is now managed as a recreational area; the underground workings are permanently sealed, but surface roads, tailings, and foundation outlines are accessible to visitors. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Sussex Mine
mines.description: The Sussex Mine came into being in 1904 when assays of dark galena boulders revealed silver values that convinced a cautious mining company to commit full capital to the district. The ore ran high in silver with notable lead credits, and the smelter's payment schedule for lead kept the mine solvent during periods when silver prices weakened. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. Fourth of July celebrations at the mine drew miners from a fifty-mile radius, the day marked by drilling competitions, mule races, and the communal roasting of beef over wood fires. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. By final closure the mine had yielded $182,261 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Preservation efforts have protected the most significant surface structures; the mine is listed on state and national historic registers and continues to attract researchers studying the industrial archaeology of western mining. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Bates Hunter Mine
mines.description: The Bates Hunter Mine was incorporated in 1866 following a season of intensive surface sampling that convinced its organizers the deposit was large enough to justify a major capital investment. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. A union organizer arrived in the spring of the mine's busiest year and spent months carefully building a membership base before calling the strike that ultimately improved wage rates across the whole district. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. The mine's cumulative output of $104,840 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Little remains above ground: the concrete foundation of the shaft house, the long gray ribbon of tailings, and a few ore-bin timbers slowly returning to the soil. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Black Hawk Mine
mines.description: A party of Welsh hard-rock miners who arrived in 1907 recognized the geology at once — the pyritized schist, the carbonate alteration — and staked the Black Hawk Mine before the week was out. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. Fourth of July celebrations at the mine drew miners from a fifty-mile radius, the day marked by drilling competitions, mule races, and the communal roasting of beef over wood fires. The ore shoots plunged steeply to the northeast, meaning that the richest ground was always at the deepest accessible level — a geological reality that drove ever-deeper shaft sinking. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. Total recorded production reached $74,277 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. Little remains above ground: the concrete foundation of the shaft house, the long gray ribbon of tailings, and a few ore-bin timbers slowly returning to the soil. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Bob Tail Mine
mines.description: A government geologist mapping the district in 1887 noted the gold-bearing structure in his survey report; within months, private capital had organized the Bob Tail Mine and begun sinking. Telluride minerals — calaverite and sylvanite — appeared at depth alongside the free-gold ore, and their assay values sometimes exceeded the free-milling upper levels. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. Miners were paid by the contract system in the early years, each man responsible for a set amount of advance per shift; the system rewarded skill and speed but created fierce competition and occasional shortcuts on safety. The ore body was controlled by a northwest-trending fault that focused hydrothermal fluids against an impermeable diorite dike — classic structural geology that the mine's long-time geologist had predicted before the drill proved it. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. The mine's cumulative output of $256,800 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The region's mining heritage tourism industry has found the Bob Tail Mine site to be one of its most evocative stops, combining accessible ruins with a well-documented historical record. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Casey Mine
mines.description: A chance glint of free gold in the tailings of an abandoned hydraulic pit led to the founding of the Casey Mine in 1868, launching what would become one of the district's longer-lived operations. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. The mine's Chinese workers, hired for surface and timbering tasks at wages below those of white miners, formed a self-contained community at the edge of camp and maintained ties to a Chinese-operated store in the nearest town. The discovery of a second ore body, separated from the main vein by a barren porphyry dike, was made by an experienced shifter who noticed a change in wall-rock alteration that others had dismissed. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. Production records show $238,606 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The site draws rockhounds and history buffs; the tailings still yield fragments of the original ore, and the preserved headframe serves as a landmark visible for miles. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Chain Mine
mines.description: When the placer gold played out upstream in 1864, savvy prospectors followed the eroded channel to its source and established the Chain Mine on the bedrock reef. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. Women's contributions to the camp economy were substantial and often overlooked; they ran boarding houses, laundries, and small stores that collectively processed more money than the mine itself in some lean years. A winter avalanche destroyed the lower section of the aerial tramway and isolated the mine from its mill for seven weeks; the company used the forced downtime to rehabilitate the underground ventilation system. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The mine's cumulative output of $266,871 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The ruins of the mill foundation, the flat ribbon of gray tailings, and the gated collar of the main shaft are all that remain of a property that once employed hundreds. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Fisk Mine
mines.description: The Fisk Mine began as a placer strike in 1873: a forty-niner scooping creek gravel noticed colors rich enough to warrant hard-rock follow-up, and within months a shaft was sinking. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. Miners were paid by the contract system in the early years, each man responsible for a set amount of advance per shift; the system rewarded skill and speed but created fierce competition and occasional shortcuts on safety. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Total recorded production reached $235,092 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and rusting machinery form an open-air industrial museum that attracts photographers and history enthusiasts. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Gunnell Mine
mines.description: When the placer gold played out upstream in 1881, savvy prospectors followed the eroded channel to its source and established the Gunnell Mine on the bedrock reef. Telluride minerals — calaverite and sylvanite — appeared at depth alongside the free-gold ore, and their assay values sometimes exceeded the free-milling upper levels. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. Labor relations were tense through much of the mine's life; the Western Federation of Miners organized the workforce in the early twentieth century, and a series of strikes tested management's resolve. A cave of the hanging wall at the deepest level sent shock waves through the entire underground workings and brought men scrambling from every portal, but miraculously caused no fatalities. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The mine's cumulative output of $262,966 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Hidden Hand Mine
mines.description: The Hidden Hand Mine opened in 1885 after a grizzled veteran spotted the telltale iron-stained gossan capping a rich lode — the kind of surface sign that experienced eyes never miss. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. The mine's machine shop could fabricate most replacement parts on-site, reducing the costly delays that plagued more remote operations dependent on long supply chains. The workforce was drawn from across the mining West: Cornish 'Cousin Jacks' ran the drilling crews, Irish muckers kept the stopes clean, Scandinavians handled the timber work, and Chinese laborers managed the most dangerous surface jobs. The ore shoots plunged steeply to the northeast, meaning that the richest ground was always at the deepest accessible level — a geological reality that drove ever-deeper shaft sinking. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. The mine's cumulative output of $48,048 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Decades after final closure, the mine's footprint — shafts, adits, tailings, mill site — remains one of the most prominent physical features in this part of the district. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Hubert Mine
mines.description: Brothers who had worked the Comstock bonanzas for a decade brought their savings and their skills to this district in 1891 and founded the Hubert Mine on a vein they traced across three separate outcroppings. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. The mine's payroll was the economic engine of the surrounding county, supporting farms, freighters, and merchants who collectively formed a community larger than the mine alone could explain. Silicification of the wallrock created a hard, persistent hanging wall that minimized dilution and reduced the need for extensive timbering in the upper levels. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. By final closure the mine had yielded $126,621 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Preservation efforts have protected the most significant surface structures; the mine is listed on state and national historic registers and continues to attract researchers studying the industrial archaeology of western mining. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Illinois Mine
mines.description: When the placer gold played out upstream in 1883, savvy prospectors followed the eroded channel to its source and established the Illinois Mine on the bedrock reef. Telluride minerals — calaverite and sylvanite — appeared at depth alongside the free-gold ore, and their assay values sometimes exceeded the free-milling upper levels. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. Veterans of the Comstock, the California camps, and the Colorado silver fields found their way here, contributing deep practical knowledge to a management that was often long on capital and short on mining experience. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The mine's cumulative output of $278,659 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The site has been partially reclaimed; the tailings were graded and seeded, the shaft capped with concrete, and the most hazardous open workings fenced — though the outline of the camp is still unmistakable from above. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Mammoth Mine
mines.description: Steam drills and organized capital defined the Mammoth Mine from its founding in 1882, when the original promoters vowed to work the deposit at a scale that hand-labor could never achieve. Free gold could be seen with the naked eye in the richest stopes, and careful mucking by experienced miners salvaged small pockets that would have been lost in a less attentive operation. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. A polyglot workforce gave the camp its distinctive character: Serbian and Croatian miners who had come from the copper mines of Central Europe occupied one end of camp, while Mexican miners who had followed silver veins north formed a tight-knit community at the other. A winter avalanche destroyed the lower section of the aerial tramway and isolated the mine from its mill for seven weeks; the company used the forced downtime to rehabilitate the underground ventilation system. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. Total recorded production reached $90,012 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. Surface structures were demolished for salvage within a decade of closure, leaving only the tailings, a few stone walls, and the collapsed collar of the main shaft to mark the location. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: New York-Bates Mine
mines.description: In 1879, a surveying crew camping on the ridge noticed yellow flecks in their fire ring stones, and the New York-Bates Mine was born from that accidental discovery. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. Underground conditions were hard: summer heat in the lower levels, constant moisture, the acrid smell of blasting powder, and ever-present silica dust that shortened miners' lives and left many in later years disabled by silicosis. The ore body was controlled by a northwest-trending fault that focused hydrothermal fluids against an impermeable diorite dike — classic structural geology that the mine's long-time geologist had predicted before the drill proved it. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. By final closure the mine had yielded $263,052 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The site has been partially reclaimed; the tailings were graded and seeded, the shaft capped with concrete, and the most hazardous open workings fenced — though the outline of the camp is still unmistakable from above. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Portland-Mackey Mine
mines.description: Gold fever brought Portland-Mackey Mine to life in 1912, when prospectors following a free-gold float trace up a creek drainage broke into a promising quartz vein and staked the first claims. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. The post office, which served the camp and surrounding district for three decades, was the connection to the wider world; its records give historians one of their most reliable windows into the composition of the workforce. Two parallel veins converged at depth into a single rich structure, a geological geometry that the mine's resident engineer had predicted on theoretical grounds but proved only after years of systematic development. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. The mine's cumulative output of $210,103 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. County historical records and a collection of mine logbooks preserved at the state historical society give researchers unusually detailed information about this property's operational history. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Quartz Hill Mine
mines.description: The Quartz Hill Mine was organized in 1880 after the district's assayer confirmed gold values that made even the most skeptical investors reach for their checkbooks. High-grade pockets produced spectacular specimens — wire gold, leaf gold, and crystalline masses — some of which were sold directly to collectors at a premium over bullion value. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. Women's contributions to the camp economy were substantial and often overlooked; they ran boarding houses, laundries, and small stores that collectively processed more money than the mine itself in some lean years. A cave of the hanging wall at the deepest level sent shock waves through the entire underground workings and brought men scrambling from every portal, but miraculously caused no fatalities. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Total recorded production reached $228,888 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. Aerial photographs reveal the full extent of the underground workings through surface subsidence patterns — shallow depressions that trace the main vein system for nearly half a mile across the hillside. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Topeka Mine
mines.description: In 1867, a surveying crew camping on the ridge noticed yellow flecks in their fire ring stones, and the Topeka Mine was born from that accidental discovery. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. The mine superintendent's house — larger and better furnished than the miners' cabins — stood on a slight rise above the camp, visible evidence of the social hierarchy that organized mining communities. The district's geology was more complex than the simple bonanza vein that early promoters had advertised; faults offset the ore body repeatedly, requiring careful surveying and systematic exploration to maintain continuity. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. The mine's cumulative output of $44,384 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Interpretive signs at the trailhead describe the mine's history for visitors exploring the surrounding public land; the actual mine site is a short walk from the parking area. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Delaware Flats Mine
mines.description: A government geologist mapping the district in 1913 noted the gold-bearing structure in his survey report; within months, private capital had organized the Delaware Flats Mine and begun sinking. High-grade pockets produced spectacular specimens — wire gold, leaf gold, and crystalline masses — some of which were sold directly to collectors at a premium over bullion value. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. A two-room schoolhouse built by the mining company's wives' auxiliary educated the district's children through all twelve grades at its peak enrollment. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. The mine was a modest operation by district standards, employing rarely more than twenty-five men and running a small stamp mill that processed ore in batches rather than continuously. The mine's cumulative output of $95,868 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: French Gulch Mine
mines.description: The French Gulch Mine was among the first deep mines in the district, sinking past the 1913 surface placers to reach the primary lode that would prove its real value. Assay values in the upper levels ran surprisingly rich in free gold, allowing the operators to ship direct to the smelter without on-site milling during the first years. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. Veterans of the Comstock, the California camps, and the Colorado silver fields found their way here, contributing deep practical knowledge to a management that was often long on capital and short on mining experience. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. Production records show $45,819 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Gold Pan Mine
mines.description: The Gold Pan Mine was staked in 1905 by a consortium of seasoned drifters who pooled their last funds to sink a test shaft — and struck the vein that justified every cent. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. A union organizer arrived in the spring of the mine's busiest year and spent months carefully building a membership base before calling the strike that ultimately improved wage rates across the whole district. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. The mine's cumulative output of $95,367 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The camp once bustled with the noise of stamps, drills, and hoists; today it is silent except for the wind, the old camp roads slowly disappearing under encroaching vegetation. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Lincoln Mine
mines.description: Steam drills and organized capital defined the Lincoln Mine from its founding in 1899, when the original promoters vowed to work the deposit at a scale that hand-labor could never achieve. Telluride minerals — calaverite and sylvanite — appeared at depth alongside the free-gold ore, and their assay values sometimes exceeded the free-milling upper levels. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. The mine superintendent's house — larger and better furnished than the miners' cabins — stood on a slight rise above the camp, visible evidence of the social hierarchy that organized mining communities. An unusually rich ore chimney was discovered when development drifts on two adjacent levels connected unexpectedly through the ore body itself, revealing a vertical pipe of native metal that astonished even veterans. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. By final closure the mine had yielded $25,788 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and rusting machinery form an open-air industrial museum that attracts photographers and history enthusiasts. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Mons Mine
mines.description: When prospectors traced a gold-bearing quartz reef up the canyon wall in 1865, the Mons Mine came into existence almost overnight, drawing capital and labor from across the territory. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. The nearby town that developed to service the mine's workforce was a rough-edged but functional community, its main street lined with frame buildings that housed every service a working miner's paycheck could buy. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. Scale was limited by the narrow width of the ore body; the mine could not employ the large crews and heavy equipment that dominated the district's major operations. Production records show $27,851 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Moonshine Mine
mines.description: A party of Welsh hard-rock miners who arrived in 1873 recognized the geology at once — the pyritized schist, the carbonate alteration — and staked the Moonshine Mine before the week was out. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. Miners were paid by the contract system in the early years, each man responsible for a set amount of advance per shift; the system rewarded skill and speed but created fierce competition and occasional shortcuts on safety. An early-morning powder explosion at the collar of the shaft leveled the headframe and blew the hoist room off its foundation, fortunately occurring during shift change when the shaft was clear of men. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. By final closure the mine had yielded $150,244 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Pug Ryan Mine
mines.description: Backed by modest Eastern capital and big local ambition, the Pug Ryan Mine sank its first shaft in 1895 and quickly proved that the quartz veins of this district ran deeper and richer than surface sampling had suggested. High-grade pockets produced spectacular specimens — wire gold, leaf gold, and crystalline masses — some of which were sold directly to collectors at a premium over bullion value. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. Veterans of the Comstock, the California camps, and the Colorado silver fields found their way here, contributing deep practical knowledge to a management that was often long on capital and short on mining experience. A runaway ore train on the inclined haulage wrecked the ore bin and damaged the crusher, shutting down production for three weeks while repairs were made and the haulage brake system redesigned. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. By final closure the mine had yielded $166,034 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Wellington Mine
mines.description: The Wellington Mine opened in 1898 after a grizzled veteran spotted the telltale iron-stained gossan capping a rich lode — the kind of surface sign that experienced eyes never miss. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. Tent cities gave way to frame structures within the first year, and within three years the camp had taken on the permanence of a real town, complete with a debating society and a brass band. A spectacular ore pocket discovered on the seventh level — massive, almost pure native metal — funded a complete renovation of the mill and hoist plant that set the mine up for another decade of profitable operation. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. Total recorded production reached $104,166 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Bonanza Mine
mines.description: Brothers who had worked the Comstock bonanzas for a decade brought their savings and their skills to this district in 1867 and founded the Bonanza Mine on a vein they traced across three separate outcroppings. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. Fourth of July celebrations at the mine drew miners from a fifty-mile radius, the day marked by drilling competitions, mule races, and the communal roasting of beef over wood fires. Fire destroyed the mill building in the mine's eleventh year; rather than rebuild the stamp mill, the company erected a modern concentrating plant that improved metal recovery significantly. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. Total recorded production reached $141,426 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The ruins of the mill foundation, the flat ribbon of gray tailings, and the gated collar of the main shaft are all that remain of a property that once employed hundreds. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Carbonateville Mine
mines.description: In 1875, a surveying crew camping on the ridge noticed yellow flecks in their fire ring stones, and the Carbonateville Mine was born from that accidental discovery. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. The mine superintendent's house — larger and better furnished than the miners' cabins — stood on a slight rise above the camp, visible evidence of the social hierarchy that organized mining communities. The ore shoots plunged steeply to the northeast, meaning that the richest ground was always at the deepest accessible level — a geological reality that drove ever-deeper shaft sinking. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. By final closure the mine had yielded $70,173 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The camp once bustled with the noise of stamps, drills, and hoists; today it is silent except for the wind, the old camp roads slowly disappearing under encroaching vegetation. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.

mines.name: Denver Mine
mines.description: A government geologist mapping the district in 1863 noted the gold-bearing structure in his survey report; within months, private capital had organized the Denver Mine and begun sinking. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. The mine's fatality rate was higher than average in its early years, reflecting both the dangerous ore body and management's tendency to prioritize production over safety improvements. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. The mine was a modest operation by district standards, employing rarely more than twenty-five men and running a small stamp mill that processed ore in batches rather than continuously. Total recorded production reached $60,766 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. Preservation efforts have protected the most significant surface structures; the mine is listed on state and national historic registers and continues to attract researchers studying the industrial archaeology of western mining. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Dyerville Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1900 on a hill already pocked with the shallow pits of earlier searchers, the Dyerville Mine went deeper and richer than any of its predecessors. The footwall side of the vein consistently ran higher in gold than the hanging wall, and experienced miners learned to work the ore selectively to maximize recovery and minimize dilution. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. The mine employed a physician during peak operations, a relatively unusual luxury that spoke to the company's interest in keeping its skilled workforce healthy and on-site. The discovery of a second ore body, separated from the main vein by a barren porphyry dike, was made by an experienced shifter who noticed a change in wall-rock alteration that others had dismissed. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. Production records show $144,085 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Illinois Mine
mines.description: The Illinois Mine dates to 1889, a period when the rush was still fresh and optimism ran high enough to justify the heavy capitalization needed to build a proper stamp mill. The oxidized zone proved exceptionally rich, but the transition to primary sulfide ore at the water table required the company to redesign its milling circuit at significant cost. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. Scale was limited by the narrow width of the ore body; the mine could not employ the large crews and heavy equipment that dominated the district's major operations. Production records show $34,421 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Aerial photographs reveal the full extent of the underground workings through surface subsidence patterns — shallow depressions that trace the main vein system for nearly half a mile across the hillside. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: King Solomon Mine
mines.description: Brothers who had worked the Comstock bonanzas for a decade brought their savings and their skills to this district in 1913 and founded the King Solomon Mine on a vein they traced across three separate outcroppings. The oxidized zone proved exceptionally rich, but the transition to primary sulfide ore at the water table required the company to redesign its milling circuit at significant cost. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. A union organizer arrived in the spring of the mine's busiest year and spent months carefully building a membership base before calling the strike that ultimately improved wage rates across the whole district. A winter avalanche destroyed the lower section of the aerial tramway and isolated the mine from its mill for seven weeks; the company used the forced downtime to rehabilitate the underground ventilation system. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. Production records show $160,772 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The property is now managed as a recreational area; the underground workings are permanently sealed, but surface roads, tailings, and foundation outlines are accessible to visitors. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.

mines.name: Washington Mine
mines.description: The Washington Mine was incorporated in 1898 on ground that Indian and Mexican miners had worked by hand for years; the new company brought steam power and ambition to what had been a subsistence operation. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. Smallpox swept the camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and forcing the temporary suspension of production until the epidemic passed and the workforce could safely reassemble. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. By final closure the mine had yielded $105,408 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. A local historical society has stabilized the surviving headframe and ore bins, and occasional guided tours bring a trickle of visitors who connect the industrial ruin to the period photographs on display inside. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Bachelor-Syracuse Mine
mines.description: A party of Welsh hard-rock miners who arrived in 1916 recognized the geology at once — the pyritized schist, the carbonate alteration — and staked the Bachelor-Syracuse Mine before the week was out. The oxidized zone proved exceptionally rich, but the transition to primary sulfide ore at the water table required the company to redesign its milling circuit at significant cost. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. The mine supported a boarding house, a company store, and a small reading room — the latter a modest concession to reform-era pressure for better living conditions in isolated mining camps. A granite porphyry intrusion had baked the surrounding sedimentary rocks into a resistant hornfels, and the skarn contact between the two formations proved to be the most productive ore zone in the district. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. The mine's cumulative output of $256,150 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Years of weathering and vandalism have reduced the surface structures to their essence, but the mine remains a recognizable industrial landscape rather than just a scar on the hillside. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Beaumont Mine
mines.description: Steam drills and organized capital defined the Beaumont Mine from its founding in 1891, when the original promoters vowed to work the deposit at a scale that hand-labor could never achieve. The footwall side of the vein consistently ran higher in gold than the hanging wall, and experienced miners learned to work the ore selectively to maximize recovery and minimize dilution. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. Wages at this mine tracked the district average closely, and the company avoided some of the worst labor conflicts of the era by maintaining steady employment through the inevitable ore-grade fluctuations. A cave of the hanging wall at the deepest level sent shock waves through the entire underground workings and brought men scrambling from every portal, but miraculously caused no fatalities. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The mine's cumulative output of $337,418 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Preservation efforts have protected the most significant surface structures; the mine is listed on state and national historic registers and continues to attract researchers studying the industrial archaeology of western mining. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.

mines.name: Ben Butler Mine
mines.description: Backed by modest Eastern capital and big local ambition, the Ben Butler Mine sank its first shaft in 1894 and quickly proved that the quartz veins of this district ran deeper and richer than surface sampling had suggested. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. A Methodist church built by mine families in the camp's early years provided a counterpoint to the saloon culture, and its congregation was a force for stability in an otherwise transient population. The district's geology was more complex than the simple bonanza vein that early promoters had advertised; faults offset the ore body repeatedly, requiring careful surveying and systematic exploration to maintain continuity. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. mines.name: Camp Bird Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1915 on a hill already pocked with the shallow pits of earlier searchers, the Camp Bird Mine went deeper and richer than any of its predecessors. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. The mine's payroll was the economic engine of the surrounding county, supporting farms, freighters, and merchants who collectively formed a community larger than the mine alone could explain. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. The mine's cumulative output of $191,126 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Interpretive signs at the trailhead describe the mine's history for visitors exploring the surrounding public land; the actual mine site is a short walk from the parking area. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.

mines.name: Mineral Farm Mine
mines.description: Gold fever brought Mineral Farm Mine to life in 1912, when prospectors following a free-gold float trace up a creek drainage broke into a promising quartz vein and staked the first claims. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. The mine's machine shop could fabricate most replacement parts on-site, reducing the costly delays that plagued more remote operations dependent on long supply chains. A Methodist church built by mine families in the camp's early years provided a counterpoint to the saloon culture, and its congregation was a force for stability in an otherwise transient population. A winter avalanche destroyed the lower section of the aerial tramway and isolated the mine from its mill for seven weeks; the company used the forced downtime to rehabilitate the underground ventilation system. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. By final closure the mine had yielded $216,627 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Little remains above ground: the concrete foundation of the shaft house, the long gray ribbon of tailings, and a few ore-bin timbers slowly returning to the soil. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Portland Mine
mines.description: When prospectors traced a gold-bearing quartz reef up the canyon wall in 1881, the Portland Mine came into existence almost overnight, drawing capital and labor from across the territory. The principal ore mineral was free-milling gold locked in quartz, simple enough to concentrate in a stamp mill and amalgamate on copper plates without expensive chemical treatment. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. Labor relations were tense through much of the mine's life; the Western Federation of Miners organized the workforce in the early twentieth century, and a series of strikes tested management's resolve. The district's geology was more complex than the simple bonanza vein that early promoters had advertised; faults offset the ore body repeatedly, requiring careful surveying and systematic exploration to maintain continuity. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Total recorded production reached $259,394 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.
