Flooding in the lower workings following an unusually wet spring required emergency pumping operations that ran for six weeks before the water level was brought back under control. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. Production records show $827,011 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. County historical records and a collection of mine logbooks preserved at the state historical society give researchers unusually detailed information about this property's operational history. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Mike Horse Mine
mines.description: In 1883, investors drawn by reports of bonanza silver ore in the district backed the founding of the Mike Horse Mine, expecting quick returns and getting a more complex ore body than they bargained for. Silver values were intimately associated with galena, and the concentrates commanded a favorable smelter schedule that rewarded careful ore preparation. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. Underground conditions were hard: summer heat in the lower levels, constant moisture, the acrid smell of blasting powder, and ever-present silica dust that shortened miners' lives and left many in later years disabled by silicosis. Country rock alterations — pervasive silicification, adularia, and bladed calcite — gave the underground workings a distinctive appearance and confirmed the high-temperature hydrothermal origin of the ore. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. The mine's cumulative output of $590,507 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and rusting machinery form an open-air industrial museum that attracts photographers and history enthusiasts. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Minnie Healey Mine
mines.description: In 1909, investors drawn by reports of bonanza silver ore in the district backed the founding of the Minnie Healey Mine, expecting quick returns and getting a more complex ore body than they bargained for. Assay grades were erratic, reflecting the pocket nature of silver-bearing ore in this district; a week of bonanza followed a month of development work on barren vein matter. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. A union organizer arrived in the spring of the mine's busiest year and spent months carefully building a membership base before calling the strike that ultimately improved wage rates across the whole district. Fire destroyed the mill building in the mine's eleventh year; rather than rebuild the stamp mill, the company erected a modern concentrating plant that improved metal recovery significantly. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. By final closure the mine had yielded $568,728 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and rusting machinery form an open-air industrial museum that attracts photographers and history enthusiasts. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Missoula Mine
mines.description: The Missoula Mine opened its first level in 1908 on a carbonate silver deposit that the local assayer valued at extraordinary grades — grades that drove frantic development through the next decade. Silver values were intimately associated with galena, and the concentrates commanded a favorable smelter schedule that rewarded careful ore preparation. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. By final closure the mine had yielded $588,299 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The site has been partially reclaimed; the tailings were graded and seeded, the shaft capped with concrete, and the most hazardous open workings fenced — though the outline of the camp is still unmistakable from above. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Montana Mine
mines.description: The Montana Mine took shape in 1892 when a systematic sampling program confirmed that the silver deposit extended far enough at depth to justify a full underground development program. Bonanza-grade pockets — native silver in massive sheets — appeared periodically in the deepest workings, funding equipment upgrades and new shaft sinking for years. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. Labor relations were tense through much of the mine's life; the Western Federation of Miners organized the workforce in the early twentieth century, and a series of strikes tested management's resolve. The ore shoots plunged steeply to the northeast, meaning that the richest ground was always at the deepest accessible level — a geological reality that drove ever-deeper shaft sinking. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. Production records show $146,591 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The site draws rockhounds and history buffs; the tailings still yield fragments of the original ore, and the preserved headframe serves as a landmark visible for miles. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Mountain Con Mine
mines.description: The Mountain Con Mine of 1886 was typical of its silver-district contemporaries: incorporated in a state capital far from the mine, run by men who trusted the geology and relied on hard Cornish miners to prove the ore body. The complex mineralogy — argentite, polybasite, and stephanite alongside common galena — required a selective milling approach that the company's metallurgist took months to perfect. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. Production records show $776,337 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Little remains above ground: the concrete foundation of the shaft house, the long gray ribbon of tailings, and a few ore-bin timbers slowly returning to the soil. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Mountain View Mine
mines.description: The Mountain View Mine was founded in 1891 on the strength of assay returns that read like fiction — bonanza-grade silver in a thick carbonate vein that crossed the entire claim. Chloride silver ore predominated in the upper oxidized zone, giving spectacular assay values that attracted early investment, but the primary sulfide ore below required a more sophisticated milling approach. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. The mine employed a physician during peak operations, a relatively unusual luxury that spoke to the company's interest in keeping its skilled workforce healthy and on-site. Flooding in the lower workings following an unusually wet spring required emergency pumping operations that ran for six weeks before the water level was brought back under control. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Production records show $402,011 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. A local historical society has stabilized the surviving headframe and ore bins, and occasional guided tours bring a trickle of visitors who connect the industrial ruin to the period photographs on display inside. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Never Sweat Mine
mines.description: A district already buzzing with silver excitement welcomed the Never Sweat Mine in 1914; the new property quickly proved that the ore body extended well beyond the surface outcrop. The ore ran high in silver with notable lead credits, and the smelter's payment schedule for lead kept the mine solvent during periods when silver prices weakened. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. The mine superintendent's house — larger and better furnished than the miners' cabins — stood on a slight rise above the camp, visible evidence of the social hierarchy that organized mining communities. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. The operation ranked among the district's largest, with a workforce exceeding two hundred in peak years and a mill complex capable of processing hundreds of tons of ore per day. Production records show $1,142,695 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The site is accessible via a graded dirt road maintained by the county, and the surviving structures are visible from the road without the need to enter the fenced hazard zone around the shaft collar. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Nettie Mine
mines.description: The Nettie Mine came into being in 1868 when assays of dark galena boulders revealed silver values that convinced a cautious mining company to commit full capital to the district. Silver values peaked in the middle levels and declined at depth, suggesting that the hydrothermal system responsible for ore deposition had a limited vertical range. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. Women's contributions to the camp economy were substantial and often overlooked; they ran boarding houses, laundries, and small stores that collectively processed more money than the mine itself in some lean years. The ore body's dip carried it under a neighboring claim, creating the legal disputes over apex rights that were common in this era and that eventually required a settlement negotiated by outside attorneys. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. Total recorded production reached $838,302 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. Today the old mine yard is quiet, the headframe long since dismantled for salvage, but the outline of the ore bins and the concrete footings of the hoist room are still legible to a practiced eye. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Nipper Mine
mines.description: When oxidized silver minerals were found beneath a gossan cap in 1889, the Nipper Mine was organized and sinking was begun before the summer was out. Silver values peaked in the middle levels and declined at depth, suggesting that the hydrothermal system responsible for ore deposition had a limited vertical range. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. Women's contributions to the camp economy were substantial and often overlooked; they ran boarding houses, laundries, and small stores that collectively processed more money than the mine itself in some lean years. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. The mine's cumulative output of $499,642 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The region's mining heritage tourism industry has found the Nipper Mine site to be one of its most evocative stops, combining accessible ruins with a well-documented historical record. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Original Mine
mines.description: Silver-lead float scattered across the hillside in 1915 told experienced prospectors exactly where to sink the first shaft of the Original Mine. Ruby silver minerals — pyrargyrite and proustite — appeared in the richest ore shoots and were sometimes preserved as specimens rather than sent to the smelter. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. Women's contributions to the camp economy were substantial and often overlooked; they ran boarding houses, laundries, and small stores that collectively processed more money than the mine itself in some lean years. Country rock alterations — pervasive silicification, adularia, and bladed calcite — gave the underground workings a distinctive appearance and confirmed the high-temperature hydrothermal origin of the ore. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. The mine's cumulative output of $924,888 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Decades after final closure, the mine's footprint — shafts, adits, tailings, mill site — remains one of the most prominent physical features in this part of the district. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Parrot Mine
mines.description: Silver chloride ore lying loose on the surface drew the first prospectors to this ground, and in 1874 the Parrot Mine was formally organized to systematically work the deposit. Bonanza-grade pockets — native silver in massive sheets — appeared periodically in the deepest workings, funding equipment upgrades and new shaft sinking for years. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. The mine's fatality rate was higher than average in its early years, reflecting both the dangerous ore body and management's tendency to prioritize production over safety improvements. A granite porphyry intrusion had baked the surrounding sedimentary rocks into a resistant hornfels, and the skarn contact between the two formations proved to be the most productive ore zone in the district. Capital investment in this property ran into the millions, funding a compressed-air drilling plant, electric hoist, and a modern flotation mill that set the standard for the district. By final closure the mine had yielded $1,148,068 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Pennsylvania Mine
mines.description: A district already buzzing with silver excitement welcomed the Pennsylvania Mine in 1873; the new property quickly proved that the ore body extended well beyond the surface outcrop. The complex mineralogy — argentite, polybasite, and stephanite alongside common galena — required a selective milling approach that the company's metallurgist took months to perfect. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. Veterans of the Comstock, the California camps, and the Colorado silver fields found their way here, contributing deep practical knowledge to a management that was often long on capital and short on mining experience. An early-morning powder explosion at the collar of the shaft leveled the headframe and blew the hoist room off its foundation, fortunately occurring during shift change when the shaft was clear of men. The operation ranked among the district's largest, with a workforce exceeding two hundred in peak years and a mill complex capable of processing hundreds of tons of ore per day. By final closure the mine had yielded $1,033,536 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. A local historical society has stabilized the surviving headframe and ore bins, and occasional guided tours bring a trickle of visitors who connect the industrial ruin to the period photographs on display inside. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.

mines.name: Pittsmont Mine
mines.description: Silver-lead float scattered across the hillside in 1907 told experienced prospectors exactly where to sink the first shaft of the Pittsmont Mine. The complex mineralogy — argentite, polybasite, and stephanite alongside common galena — required a selective milling approach that the company's metallurgist took months to perfect. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. The mine employed a physician during peak operations, a relatively unusual luxury that spoke to the company's interest in keeping its skilled workforce healthy and on-site. The vein system was part of a larger epithermal gold-silver network that geologists now understand as a single hydrothermal cell; at the time, miners just followed the ore wherever it led. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. The mine's cumulative output of $959,443 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and rusting machinery form an open-air industrial museum that attracts photographers and history enthusiasts. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Alder Gulch Mine
mines.description: Cornish miners who had drifted north from the California camps staked the Alder Gulch Mine in 1865, bringing their tradition of deep, systematic hard-rock work to what had been a surface-only district. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. Miners were paid by the contract system in the early years, each man responsible for a set amount of advance per shift; the system rewarded skill and speed but created fierce competition and occasional shortcuts on safety. A cave of the hanging wall at the deepest level sent shock waves through the entire underground workings and brought men scrambling from every portal, but miraculously caused no fatalities. The mine was a modest operation by district standards, employing rarely more than twenty-five men and running a small stamp mill that processed ore in batches rather than continuously. By final closure the mine had yielded $75,344 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Years of weathering and vandalism have reduced the surface structures to their essence, but the mine remains a recognizable industrial landscape rather than just a scar on the hillside. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Baxter Mine
mines.description: A government geologist mapping the district in 1878 noted the gold-bearing structure in his survey report; within months, private capital had organized the Baxter Mine and begun sinking. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. The company maintained a library of technical and general reading for its employees — an early example of the paternalistic amenities that larger mining corporations were beginning to offer in the reform era. The ore body's dip carried it under a neighboring claim, creating the legal disputes over apex rights that were common in this era and that eventually required a settlement negotiated by outside attorneys. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. By final closure the mine had yielded $257,984 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Decades after final closure, the mine's footprint — shafts, adits, tailings, mill site — remains one of the most prominent physical features in this part of the district. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Beutler Mine
mines.description: Steam drills and organized capital defined the Beutler Mine from its founding in 1886, when the original promoters vowed to work the deposit at a scale that hand-labor could never achieve. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. The mine superintendent's house — larger and better furnished than the miners' cabins — stood on a slight rise above the camp, visible evidence of the social hierarchy that organized mining communities. A legal dispute over apex rights with a neighboring claim lasted four years in the courts and consumed legal fees that might otherwise have funded two additional levels of development. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. Total recorded production reached $141,402 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. Interpretive signs at the trailhead describe the mine's history for visitors exploring the surrounding public land; the actual mine site is a short walk from the parking area. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Black Picard Mine
mines.description: The Black Picard Mine was staked in 1899 by a consortium of seasoned drifters who pooled their last funds to sink a test shaft — and struck the vein that justified every cent. High-grade pockets produced spectacular specimens — wire gold, leaf gold, and crystalline masses — some of which were sold directly to collectors at a premium over bullion value. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. Women's contributions to the camp economy were substantial and often overlooked; they ran boarding houses, laundries, and small stores that collectively processed more money than the mine itself in some lean years. A runaway ore train on the inclined haulage wrecked the ore bin and damaged the crusher, shutting down production for three weeks while repairs were made and the haulage brake system redesigned. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. By final closure the mine had yielded $49,068 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. A local historical society has stabilized the surviving headframe and ore bins, and occasional guided tours bring a trickle of visitors who connect the industrial ruin to the period photographs on display inside. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Bruner Mine
mines.description: In the second year of the big rush, 1872, the Bruner Mine differentiated itself from dozens of flash-in-the-pan ventures by hitting a thick, continuous ore body at only forty feet of depth. Free gold could be seen with the naked eye in the richest stopes, and careful mucking by experienced miners salvaged small pockets that would have been lost in a less attentive operation. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. A mutual aid society formed by the miners provided modest death benefits and sick pay, filling a welfare gap that the company itself was slow to address. The deposit was zoned: near-surface gold grading into silver-dominant ore at depth, with lead-zinc values increasing in the lowest levels — a telescoped epithermal system that geologists found instructive. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Total recorded production reached $230,137 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The stone assay office walls remain standing — thick enough to withstand a century of weather and neglect — while the surrounding wooden structures have long since collapsed. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Buckskin Joe Mine
mines.description: Cornish miners who had drifted north from the California camps staked the Buckskin Joe Mine in 1886, bringing their tradition of deep, systematic hard-rock work to what had been a surface-only district. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. Tent cities gave way to frame structures within the first year, and within three years the camp had taken on the permanence of a real town, complete with a debating society and a brass band. The ore body's dip carried it under a neighboring claim, creating the legal disputes over apex rights that were common in this era and that eventually required a settlement negotiated by outside attorneys. The mine was a modest operation by district standards, employing rarely more than twenty-five men and running a small stamp mill that processed ore in batches rather than continuously. Production records show $144,164 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Little remains above ground: the concrete foundation of the shaft house, the long gray ribbon of tailings, and a few ore-bin timbers slowly returning to the soil. mines.name: Cliff Mine
mines.description: The Cliff Mine dates to 1867, a period when the rush was still fresh and optimism ran high enough to justify the heavy capitalization needed to build a proper stamp mill. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. The mine supported a boarding house, a company store, and a small reading room — the latter a modest concession to reform-era pressure for better living conditions in isolated mining camps. Metamorphic aureole rocks around the intrusive complex provided the chemical trap for ore deposition, and the mine's geology bore a close resemblance to larger deposits in more famous districts. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. By final closure the mine had yielded $200,698 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. A local historical society has stabilized the surviving headframe and ore bins, and occasional guided tours bring a trickle of visitors who connect the industrial ruin to the period photographs on display inside. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Daylight Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1896 by a stock company formed in the state capital, the Daylight Mine quickly justified its investors' faith when the first deep level revealed ore far richer than the near-surface sampling had suggested. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. The mine superintendent's house — larger and better furnished than the miners' cabins — stood on a slight rise above the camp, visible evidence of the social hierarchy that organized mining communities. Metamorphic aureole rocks around the intrusive complex provided the chemical trap for ore deposition, and the mine's geology bore a close resemblance to larger deposits in more famous districts. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. By final closure the mine had yielded $142,746 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The stone assay office walls remain standing — thick enough to withstand a century of weather and neglect — while the surrounding wooden structures have long since collapsed. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Duffield Mine
mines.description: The Duffield Mine was incorporated in 1883 on ground that Indian and Mexican miners had worked by hand for years; the new company brought steam power and ambition to what had been a subsistence operation. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. The mine's machine shop could fabricate most replacement parts on-site, reducing the costly delays that plagued more remote operations dependent on long supply chains. A union organizer arrived in the spring of the mine's busiest year and spent months carefully building a membership base before calling the strike that ultimately improved wage rates across the whole district. An early-morning powder explosion at the collar of the shaft leveled the headframe and blew the hoist room off its foundation, fortunately occurring during shift change when the shaft was clear of men. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Production records show $221,877 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Fritz Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1878 on a hill already pocked with the shallow pits of earlier searchers, the Fritz Mine went deeper and richer than any of its predecessors. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. A Methodist church built by mine families in the camp's early years provided a counterpoint to the saloon culture, and its congregation was a force for stability in an otherwise transient population. Country rock alterations — pervasive silicification, adularia, and bladed calcite — gave the underground workings a distinctive appearance and confirmed the high-temperature hydrothermal origin of the ore. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. Total recorded production reached $308,946 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The surface plant has mostly vanished, but the mine's legacy persists in county records, family histories, and the occasional specimen jar of ore that turns up at a local antique fair. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Gold Bug Mine
mines.description: Gold fever brought Gold Bug Mine to life in 1864, when prospectors following a free-gold float trace up a creek drainage broke into a promising quartz vein and staked the first claims. Assay values in the upper levels ran surprisingly rich in free gold, allowing the operators to ship direct to the smelter without on-site milling during the first years. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. Family men who planned to stay rather than drift formed the backbone of the camp's permanent population, building small farms on nearby land and sending their children to the district school. A granite porphyry intrusion had baked the surrounding sedimentary rocks into a resistant hornfels, and the skarn contact between the two formations proved to be the most productive ore zone in the district. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. Production records show $245,989 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Interpretive signs at the trailhead describe the mine's history for visitors exploring the surrounding public land; the actual mine site is a short walk from the parking area. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Green Fancy Mine
mines.description: When the placer gold played out upstream in 1894, savvy prospectors followed the eroded channel to its source and established the Green Fancy Mine on the bedrock reef. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. A polyglot workforce gave the camp its distinctive character: Serbian and Croatian miners who had come from the copper mines of Central Europe occupied one end of camp, while Mexican miners who had followed silver veins north formed a tight-knit community at the other. Smallpox swept the camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and forcing the temporary suspension of production until the epidemic passed and the workforce could safely reassemble. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. By final closure the mine had yielded $184,969 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. A state historic marker at the road junction explains the mine's significance; visitors who venture up the old access road find the ruins more extensive than the marker suggests. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Gulch Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1898 by a stock company formed in the state capital, the Gulch Mine quickly justified its investors' faith when the first deep level revealed ore far richer than the near-surface sampling had suggested. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. A small town grew up around the mine: a post office, a two-room schoolhouse, three saloons, and the inevitable assay office that doubled as banker and source of local credit. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. Total recorded production reached $72,973 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. Years of weathering and vandalism have reduced the surface structures to their essence, but the mine remains a recognizable industrial landscape rather than just a scar on the hillside. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Harvey Mine
mines.description: The Harvey Mine dates to 1879, a period when the rush was still fresh and optimism ran high enough to justify the heavy capitalization needed to build a proper stamp mill. Telluride minerals — calaverite and sylvanite — appeared at depth alongside the free-gold ore, and their assay values sometimes exceeded the free-milling upper levels. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. Tent cities gave way to frame structures within the first year, and within three years the camp had taken on the permanence of a real town, complete with a debating society and a brass band. The discovery of a second ore body, separated from the main vein by a barren porphyry dike, was made by an experienced shifter who noticed a change in wall-rock alteration that others had dismissed. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Total recorded production reached $237,131 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. Years of weathering and vandalism have reduced the surface structures to their essence, but the mine remains a recognizable industrial landscape rather than just a scar on the hillside. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.

mines.name: Henry Mine
mines.description: Gold fever brought Henry Mine to life in 1876, when prospectors following a free-gold float trace up a creek drainage broke into a promising quartz vein and staked the first claims. The principal ore mineral was free-milling gold locked in quartz, simple enough to concentrate in a stamp mill and amalgamate on copper plates without expensive chemical treatment. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. Flooding in the lower workings following an unusually wet spring required emergency pumping operations that ran for six weeks before the water level was brought back under control. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. The mine's cumulative output of $318,982 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The stone assay office walls remain standing — thick enough to withstand a century of weather and neglect — while the surrounding wooden structures have long since collapsed. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Highland Mine
mines.description: The Highland Mine opened in 1881 after a grizzled veteran spotted the telltale iron-stained gossan capping a rich lode — the kind of surface sign that experienced eyes never miss. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. The mine supported a boarding house, a company store, and a small reading room — the latter a modest concession to reform-era pressure for better living conditions in isolated mining camps. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Total recorded production reached $234,315 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The region's mining heritage tourism industry has found the Highland Mine site to be one of its most evocative stops, combining accessible ruins with a well-documented historical record. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Jack Pot Mine
mines.description: The Jack Pot Mine was among the first deep mines in the district, sinking past the 1894 surface placers to reach the primary lode that would prove its real value. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. The post office, which served the camp and surrounding district for three decades, was the connection to the wider world; its records give historians one of their most reliable windows into the composition of the workforce. Metamorphic aureole rocks around the intrusive complex provided the chemical trap for ore deposition, and the mine's geology bore a close resemblance to larger deposits in more famous districts. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. By final closure the mine had yielded $96,050 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The site has been partially reclaimed; the tailings were graded and seeded, the shaft capped with concrete, and the most hazardous open workings fenced — though the outline of the camp is still unmistakable from above. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Jackson Mine
mines.description: When prospectors traced a gold-bearing quartz reef up the canyon wall in 1871, the Jackson Mine came into existence almost overnight, drawing capital and labor from across the territory. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. Underground conditions were hard: summer heat in the lower levels, constant moisture, the acrid smell of blasting powder, and ever-present silica dust that shortened miners' lives and left many in later years disabled by silicosis. An unusually rich ore chimney was discovered when development drifts on two adjacent levels connected unexpectedly through the ore body itself, revealing a vertical pipe of native metal that astonished even veterans. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The mine's cumulative output of $278,791 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The ruins of the mill foundation, the flat ribbon of gray tailings, and the gated collar of the main shaft are all that remain of a property that once employed hundreds. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Jefferson Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1891 on a hill already pocked with the shallow pits of earlier searchers, the Jefferson Mine went deeper and richer than any of its predecessors. The principal ore mineral was free-milling gold locked in quartz, simple enough to concentrate in a stamp mill and amalgamate on copper plates without expensive chemical treatment. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. Wages at this mine tracked the district average closely, and the company avoided some of the worst labor conflicts of the era by maintaining steady employment through the inevitable ore-grade fluctuations. Metamorphic aureole rocks around the intrusive complex provided the chemical trap for ore deposition, and the mine's geology bore a close resemblance to larger deposits in more famous districts. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. Production records show $88,344 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Decades after final closure, the mine's footprint — shafts, adits, tailings, mill site — remains one of the most prominent physical features in this part of the district. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Johnson Mine
mines.description: The Johnson Mine began as a placer strike in 1908: a forty-niner scooping creek gravel noticed colors rich enough to warrant hard-rock follow-up, and within months a shaft was sinking. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. A union organizer arrived in the spring of the mine's busiest year and spent months carefully building a membership base before calling the strike that ultimately improved wage rates across the whole district. An early-morning powder explosion at the collar of the shaft leveled the headframe and blew the hoist room off its foundation, fortunately occurring during shift change when the shaft was clear of men. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. By final closure the mine had yielded $164,631 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.

mines.name: June Bug Mine
mines.description: Steam drills and organized capital defined the June Bug Mine from its founding in 1868, when the original promoters vowed to work the deposit at a scale that hand-labor could never achieve. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. The mine supported a boarding house, a company store, and a small reading room — the latter a modest concession to reform-era pressure for better living conditions in isolated mining camps. The ore shoots plunged steeply to the northeast, meaning that the richest ground was always at the deepest accessible level — a geological reality that drove ever-deeper shaft sinking. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The mine's cumulative output of $266,944 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The site draws rockhounds and history buffs; the tailings still yield fragments of the original ore, and the preserved headframe serves as a landmark visible for miles. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Keeley Mine
mines.description: A chance glint of free gold in the tailings of an abandoned hydraulic pit led to the founding of the Keeley Mine in 1883, launching what would become one of the district's longer-lived operations. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. Labor relations were tense through much of the mine's life; the Western Federation of Miners organized the workforce in the early twentieth century, and a series of strikes tested management's resolve. During a record snowfall winter, supply wagons could not reach the mine for six weeks, and the crew worked on reduced rations until the road was reopened — a testament to the determination of the men and their families. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. Production records show $226,995 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The stone assay office walls remain standing — thick enough to withstand a century of weather and neglect — while the surrounding wooden structures have long since collapsed. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.

mines.name: Kent Mine
mines.description: Gold fever brought Kent Mine to life in 1908, when prospectors following a free-gold float trace up a creek drainage broke into a promising quartz vein and staked the first claims. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. A labor walkout in the mine's peak year idled the property for nearly three months, during which the owners brought in outside contractors who struggled to maintain the complex underground water balance. The mine was a modest operation by district standards, employing rarely more than twenty-five men and running a small stamp mill that processed ore in batches rather than continuously. The mine's cumulative output of $58,183 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The site draws rockhounds and history buffs; the tailings still yield fragments of the original ore, and the preserved headframe serves as a landmark visible for miles. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.

mines.description: The Kimball Mine traces its origins to 1897, when a prospector whose horse stumbled on the hillside noticed the exposed quartz float and recognized the classic markers of a primary gold deposit. The mine was a modest operation by district standards, employing rarely more than twenty-five men and running a small stamp mill that processed ore in batches rather than continuously. Preservation efforts have protected the most significant surface structures; the mine is listed on state and national historic registers and continues to attract researchers studying the industrial archaeology of western mining. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.

mines.description: In 1882, a surveying crew camping on the ridge noticed yellow flecks in their fire ring stones, and the Kirk Mine was born from that accidental discovery. The company maintained a library of technical and general reading for its employees — an early example of the paternalistic amenities that larger mining corporations were beginning to offer in the reform era. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. Miners were paid by the contract system in the early years, each man responsible for a set amount of advance per shift; the system rewarded skill and speed but created fierce competition and occasional shortcuts on safety. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. By final closure the mine had yielded $121,576 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The site is accessible via a graded dirt road maintained by the county, and the surviving structures are visible from the road without the need to enter the fenced hazard zone around the shaft collar. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Fielding Mine
mines.description: The Fielding Mine was staked in 1869 by a consortium of seasoned drifters who pooled their last funds to sink a test shaft — and struck the vein that justified every cent. The principal ore mineral was free-milling gold locked in quartz, simple enough to concentrate in a stamp mill and amalgamate on copper plates without expensive chemical treatment. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. The mine's machine shop could fabricate most replacement parts on-site, reducing the costly delays that plagued more remote operations dependent on long supply chains. A Methodist church built by mine families in the camp's early years provided a counterpoint to the saloon culture, and its congregation was a force for stability in an otherwise transient population. A legal dispute over apex rights with a neighboring claim lasted four years in the courts and consumed legal fees that might otherwise have funded two additional levels of development. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The mine's cumulative output of $207,324 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. A concrete collar caps the main shaft, and warning signs mark the perimeter of the historic workings, but visitors are free to walk the surrounding grounds and examine the extensive tailings. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Gold Creek Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1870 by a stock company formed in the state capital, the Gold Creek Mine quickly justified its investors' faith when the first deep level revealed ore far richer than the near-surface sampling had suggested. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. Wages at this mine tracked the district average closely, and the company avoided some of the worst labor conflicts of the era by maintaining steady employment through the inevitable ore-grade fluctuations. The ore body was controlled by a northwest-trending fault that focused hydrothermal fluids against an impermeable diorite dike — classic structural geology that the mine's long-time geologist had predicted before the drill proved it. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. Production records show $72,613 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. County historical records and a collection of mine logbooks preserved at the state historical society give researchers unusually detailed information about this property's operational history. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Henry Plummer Mine
mines.description: Winter did not stop the founding of the Henry Plummer Mine; in 1893, men working frozen ground with picks and bars broke into a gold-bearing structure that would define the next two decades. Assay values in the upper levels ran surprisingly rich in free gold, allowing the operators to ship direct to the smelter without on-site milling during the first years. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. A small town grew up around the mine: a post office, a two-room schoolhouse, three saloons, and the inevitable assay office that doubled as banker and source of local credit. A labor walkout in the mine's peak year idled the property for nearly three months, during which the owners brought in outside contractors who struggled to maintain the complex underground water balance. Scale was limited by the narrow width of the ore body; the mine could not employ the large crews and heavy equipment that dominated the district's major operations. Total recorded production reached $34,731 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The property is now managed as a recreational area; the underground workings are permanently sealed, but surface roads, tailings, and foundation outlines are accessible to visitors. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
