mines.name: Galena Mine
mines.description: A government geologist mapping the district in 1907 noted the gold-bearing structure in his survey report; within months, private capital had organized the Galena Mine and begun sinking. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. The mine supported a boarding house, a company store, and a small reading room — the latter a modest concession to reform-era pressure for better living conditions in isolated mining camps. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. The mine was a modest operation by district standards, employing rarely more than twenty-five men and running a small stamp mill that processed ore in batches rather than continuously. The mine's cumulative output of $120,279 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Today the old mine yard is quiet, the headframe long since dismantled for salvage, but the outline of the ore bins and the concrete footings of the hoist room are still legible to a practiced eye. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Granite Mine
mines.description: In 1893, a surveying crew camping on the ridge noticed yellow flecks in their fire ring stones, and the Granite Mine was born from that accidental discovery. Assay values in the upper levels ran surprisingly rich in free gold, allowing the operators to ship direct to the smelter without on-site milling during the first years. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. Family men who planned to stay rather than drift formed the backbone of the camp's permanent population, building small farms on nearby land and sending their children to the district school. A labor walkout in the mine's peak year idled the property for nearly three months, during which the owners brought in outside contractors who struggled to maintain the complex underground water balance. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. By final closure the mine had yielded $50,324 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Interpretive signs at the trailhead describe the mine's history for visitors exploring the surrounding public land; the actual mine site is a short walk from the parking area. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Green Gulch Mine
mines.description: The Green Gulch Mine was among the first deep mines in the district, sinking past the 1867 surface placers to reach the primary lode that would prove its real value. Telluride minerals — calaverite and sylvanite — appeared at depth alongside the free-gold ore, and their assay values sometimes exceeded the free-milling upper levels. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. Fourth of July celebrations at the mine drew miners from a fifty-mile radius, the day marked by drilling competitions, mule races, and the communal roasting of beef over wood fires. A runaway ore train on the inclined haulage wrecked the ore bin and damaged the crusher, shutting down production for three weeks while repairs were made and the haulage brake system redesigned. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. The mine's cumulative output of $68,197 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Little remains above ground: the concrete foundation of the shaft house, the long gray ribbon of tailings, and a few ore-bin timbers slowly returning to the soil. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Helena Mine
mines.description: When prospectors traced a gold-bearing quartz reef up the canyon wall in 1890, the Helena Mine came into existence almost overnight, drawing capital and labor from across the territory. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. Underground conditions were hard: summer heat in the lower levels, constant moisture, the acrid smell of blasting powder, and ever-present silica dust that shortened miners' lives and left many in later years disabled by silicosis. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. Scale was limited by the narrow width of the ore body; the mine could not employ the large crews and heavy equipment that dominated the district's major operations. Total recorded production reached $136,693 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. Little remains above ground: the concrete foundation of the shaft house, the long gray ribbon of tailings, and a few ore-bin timbers slowly returning to the soil. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Highland Mine
mines.description: When prospectors traced a gold-bearing quartz reef up the canyon wall in 1877, the Highland Mine came into existence almost overnight, drawing capital and labor from across the territory. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. The mine supported a boarding house, a company store, and a small reading room — the latter a modest concession to reform-era pressure for better living conditions in isolated mining camps. During a record snowfall winter, supply wagons could not reach the mine for six weeks, and the crew worked on reduced rations until the road was reopened — a testament to the determination of the men and their families. Scale was limited by the narrow width of the ore body; the mine could not employ the large crews and heavy equipment that dominated the district's major operations. Total recorded production reached $106,440 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The camp once bustled with the noise of stamps, drills, and hoists; today it is silent except for the wind, the old camp roads slowly disappearing under encroaching vegetation. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Last Chance Gulch Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1873 on a hill already pocked with the shallow pits of earlier searchers, the Last Chance Gulch Mine went deeper and richer than any of its predecessors. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. The mine's fatality rate was higher than average in its early years, reflecting both the dangerous ore body and management's tendency to prioritize production over safety improvements. Metamorphic aureole rocks around the intrusive complex provided the chemical trap for ore deposition, and the mine's geology bore a close resemblance to larger deposits in more famous districts. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. Production records show $100,642 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The ruins of the mill foundation, the flat ribbon of gray tailings, and the gated collar of the main shaft are all that remain of a property that once employed hundreds. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Montana Consolidated Mine
mines.description: A party of Welsh hard-rock miners who arrived in 1909 recognized the geology at once — the pyritized schist, the carbonate alteration — and staked the Montana Consolidated Mine before the week was out. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. The mine's fatality rate was higher than average in its early years, reflecting both the dangerous ore body and management's tendency to prioritize production over safety improvements. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. Production records show $102,056 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. Surface structures were demolished for salvage within a decade of closure, leaving only the tailings, a few stone walls, and the collapsed collar of the main shaft to mark the location. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Bi-Metallic Mine
mines.description: Galena float ore rich in silver had been noted for years before anyone organized the Bi-Metallic Mine in 1889 and put systematic capital behind proving the deposit. The ore ran high in silver with notable lead credits, and the smelter's payment schedule for lead kept the mine solvent during periods when silver prices weakened. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. A two-room schoolhouse built by the mining company's wives' auxiliary educated the district's children through all twelve grades at its peak enrollment. The vein system was part of a larger epithermal gold-silver network that geologists now understand as a single hydrothermal cell; at the time, miners just followed the ore wherever it led. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Total recorded production reached $352,243 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and rusting machinery form an open-air industrial museum that attracts photographers and history enthusiasts. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Black Pine Mine
mines.description: The Black Pine Mine was founded in 1875 on the strength of assay returns that read like fiction — bonanza-grade silver in a thick carbonate vein that crossed the entire claim. The carbonate silver ore of the upper levels gave way at depth to a complex sulfide assemblage that required roasting before conventional amalgamation could recover the silver. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. Fourth of July celebrations at the mine drew miners from a fifty-mile radius, the day marked by drilling competitions, mule races, and the communal roasting of beef over wood fires. An early-morning powder explosion at the collar of the shaft leveled the headframe and blew the hoist room off its foundation, fortunately occurring during shift change when the shaft was clear of men. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. The mine's cumulative output of $284,609 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The stone assay office walls remain standing — thick enough to withstand a century of weather and neglect — while the surrounding wooden structures have long since collapsed. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Blue Bird Mine
mines.description: The Blue Bird Mine of 1899 was typical of its silver-district contemporaries: incorporated in a state capital far from the mine, run by men who trusted the geology and relied on hard Cornish miners to prove the ore body. The ore ran high in silver with notable lead credits, and the smelter's payment schedule for lead kept the mine solvent during periods when silver prices weakened. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. The mine's fatality rate was higher than average in its early years, reflecting both the dangerous ore body and management's tendency to prioritize production over safety improvements. The discovery of a second ore body, separated from the main vein by a barren porphyry dike, was made by an experienced shifter who noticed a change in wall-rock alteration that others had dismissed. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. The mine's cumulative output of $221,920 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The site draws rockhounds and history buffs; the tailings still yield fragments of the original ore, and the preserved headframe serves as a landmark visible for miles. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Boulder Mine
mines.description: The Boulder Mine was staked in 1887 after chloride silver ore — rich, brittle, and unmistakable — was found cropping out of a gully wall that spring floods had freshly cut. Bonanza-grade pockets — native silver in massive sheets — appeared periodically in the deepest workings, funding equipment upgrades and new shaft sinking for years. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. The mine's fatality rate was higher than average in its early years, reflecting both the dangerous ore body and management's tendency to prioritize production over safety improvements. A cave-in on the fourth level trapped fourteen men for thirty-six hours before rescuers broke through from an adjacent drift, an event that prompted the company to adopt a more rigorous ground-support standard. The mine was a modest operation by district standards, employing rarely more than twenty-five men and running a small stamp mill that processed ore in batches rather than continuously. By final closure the mine had yielded $121,147 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Decades after final closure, the mine's footprint — shafts, adits, tailings, mill site — remains one of the most prominent physical features in this part of the district. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Collins Mine
mines.description: When oxidized silver minerals were found beneath a gossan cap in 1874, the Collins Mine was organized and sinking was begun before the summer was out. Silver values peaked in the middle levels and declined at depth, suggesting that the hydrothermal system responsible for ore deposition had a limited vertical range. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. Veterans of the Comstock, the California camps, and the Colorado silver fields found their way here, contributing deep practical knowledge to a management that was often long on capital and short on mining experience. A labor walkout in the mine's peak year idled the property for nearly three months, during which the owners brought in outside contractors who struggled to maintain the complex underground water balance. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. The mine's cumulative output of $129,254 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Commonwealth Mine
mines.description: The Commonwealth Mine opened its first level in 1882 on a carbonate silver deposit that the local assayer valued at extraordinary grades — grades that drove frantic development through the next decade. Assay grades were erratic, reflecting the pocket nature of silver-bearing ore in this district; a week of bonanza followed a month of development work on barren vein matter. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. The mine's machine shop could fabricate most replacement parts on-site, reducing the costly delays that plagued more remote operations dependent on long supply chains. Miners were paid by the contract system in the early years, each man responsible for a set amount of advance per shift; the system rewarded skill and speed but created fierce competition and occasional shortcuts on safety. Country rock alterations — pervasive silicification, adularia, and bladed calcite — gave the underground workings a distinctive appearance and confirmed the high-temperature hydrothermal origin of the ore. Scale was limited by the narrow width of the ore body; the mine could not employ the large crews and heavy equipment that dominated the district's major operations. By final closure the mine had yielded $89,376 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Little remains above ground: the concrete foundation of the shaft house, the long gray ribbon of tailings, and a few ore-bin timbers slowly returning to the soil. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Cordova Mine
mines.description: The Cordova Mine was staked in 1870 after chloride silver ore — rich, brittle, and unmistakable — was found cropping out of a gully wall that spring floods had freshly cut. The silver occurred chiefly as argentite and native silver in a carbonate gangue, making hand-sorting practical and giving high-grade ore shipments an attractive economic profile. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. Two parallel veins converged at depth into a single rich structure, a geological geometry that the mine's resident engineer had predicted on theoretical grounds but proved only after years of systematic development. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. By final closure the mine had yielded $136,417 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Surface structures were demolished for salvage within a decade of closure, leaving only the tailings, a few stone walls, and the collapsed collar of the main shaft to mark the location. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Delmonte Mine
mines.description: The Delmonte Mine was staked in 1891 after chloride silver ore — rich, brittle, and unmistakable — was found cropping out of a gully wall that spring floods had freshly cut. The carbonate silver ore of the upper levels gave way at depth to a complex sulfide assemblage that required roasting before conventional amalgamation could recover the silver. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. Fourth of July celebrations at the mine drew miners from a fifty-mile radius, the day marked by drilling competitions, mule races, and the communal roasting of beef over wood fires. A winter avalanche destroyed the lower section of the aerial tramway and isolated the mine from its mill for seven weeks; the company used the forced downtime to rehabilitate the underground ventilation system. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. The mine's cumulative output of $317,212 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The site draws rockhounds and history buffs; the tailings still yield fragments of the original ore, and the preserved headframe serves as a landmark visible for miles. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Douglas Mine
mines.description: A traveling assayer's offhand remark about the silver content of rocks near a mountain spring set off a staking rush in 1893 that placed the Douglas Mine at its center. The silver occurred chiefly as argentite and native silver in a carbonate gangue, making hand-sorting practical and giving high-grade ore shipments an attractive economic profile. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. Tent cities gave way to frame structures within the first year, and within three years the camp had taken on the permanence of a real town, complete with a debating society and a brass band. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. The mine was a modest operation by district standards, employing rarely more than twenty-five men and running a small stamp mill that processed ore in batches rather than continuously. By final closure the mine had yielded $123,205 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and rusting machinery form an open-air industrial museum that attracts photographers and history enthusiasts. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.

mines.name: Flint Creek Mine
mines.description: A traveling assayer's offhand remark about the silver content of rocks near a mountain spring set off a staking rush in 1877 that placed the Flint Creek Mine at its center. Silver values were intimately associated with galena, and the concentrates commanded a favorable smelter schedule that rewarded careful ore preparation. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. Veterans of the Comstock, the California camps, and the Colorado silver fields found their way here, contributing deep practical knowledge to a management that was often long on capital and short on mining experience. The ore shoots plunged steeply to the northeast, meaning that the richest ground was always at the deepest accessible level — a geological reality that drove ever-deeper shaft sinking. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. By final closure the mine had yielded $301,246 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Interpretive signs at the trailhead describe the mine's history for visitors exploring the surrounding public land; the actual mine site is a short walk from the parking area. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.

mines.name: Granite Mountain Mine
mines.description: Galena float ore rich in silver had been noted for years before anyone organized the Granite Mountain Mine in 1903 and put systematic capital behind proving the deposit. Assay grades were erratic, reflecting the pocket nature of silver-bearing ore in this district; a week of bonanza followed a month of development work on barren vein matter. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. Fourth of July celebrations at the mine drew miners from a fifty-mile radius, the day marked by drilling competitions, mule races, and the communal roasting of beef over wood fires. A cave of the hanging wall at the deepest level sent shock waves through the entire underground workings and brought men scrambling from every portal, but miraculously caused no fatalities. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Production records show $272,155 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and rusting machinery form an open-air industrial museum that attracts photographers and history enthusiasts. mines.name: Hope Mine
mines.description: The Hope Mine of 1879 was typical of its silver-district contemporaries: incorporated in a state capital far from the mine, run by men who trusted the geology and relied on hard Cornish miners to prove the ore body. Bonanza-grade pockets — native silver in massive sheets — appeared periodically in the deepest workings, funding equipment upgrades and new shaft sinking for years. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. Tent cities gave way to frame structures within the first year, and within three years the camp had taken on the permanence of a real town, complete with a debating society and a brass band. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The mine's cumulative output of $285,383 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The site has been partially reclaimed; the tailings were graded and seeded, the shaft capped with concrete, and the most hazardous open workings fenced — though the outline of the camp is still unmistakable from above. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Independence Mine
mines.description: Silver-lead float scattered across the hillside in 1898 told experienced prospectors exactly where to sink the first shaft of the Independence Mine. The complex mineralogy — argentite, polybasite, and stephanite alongside common galena — required a selective milling approach that the company's metallurgist took months to perfect. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. A union organizer arrived in the spring of the mine's busiest year and spent months carefully building a membership base before calling the strike that ultimately improved wage rates across the whole district. Flooding in the lower workings following an unusually wet spring required emergency pumping operations that ran for six weeks before the water level was brought back under control. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Total recorded production reached $358,730 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. A local historical society has stabilized the surviving headframe and ore bins, and occasional guided tours bring a trickle of visitors who connect the industrial ruin to the period photographs on display inside. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Noonday Mine
mines.description: The Noonday Mine came into being in 1896 when assays of dark galena boulders revealed silver values that convinced a cautious mining company to commit full capital to the district. Bonanza-grade pockets — native silver in massive sheets — appeared periodically in the deepest workings, funding equipment upgrades and new shaft sinking for years. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. The workforce was drawn from across the mining West: Cornish 'Cousin Jacks' ran the drilling crews, Irish muckers kept the stopes clean, Scandinavians handled the timber work, and Chinese laborers managed the most dangerous surface jobs. The ore shoots plunged steeply to the northeast, meaning that the richest ground was always at the deepest accessible level — a geological reality that drove ever-deeper shaft sinking. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Total recorded production reached $202,109 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. Surface structures were demolished for salvage within a decade of closure, leaving only the tailings, a few stone walls, and the collapsed collar of the main shaft to mark the location. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.

mines.name: Stonewall Mine
mines.description: The Stonewall Mine was staked in 1886 after chloride silver ore — rich, brittle, and unmistakable — was found cropping out of a gully wall that spring floods had freshly cut. The silver occurred chiefly as argentite and native silver in a carbonate gangue, making hand-sorting practical and giving high-grade ore shipments an attractive economic profile. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. The mine supported a boarding house, a company store, and a small reading room — the latter a modest concession to reform-era pressure for better living conditions in isolated mining camps. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. Total recorded production reached $201,672 estimated historical value, a figure that placed the mine firmly in the second tier of district producers. The surface plant has mostly vanished, but the mine's legacy persists in county records, family histories, and the occasional specimen jar of ore that turns up at a local antique fair. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Elkhorn Belle Mine
mines.description: The Elkhorn Belle Mine was incorporated in 1877 on ground that Indian and Mexican miners had worked by hand for years; the new company brought steam power and ambition to what had been a subsistence operation. High-grade pockets produced spectacular specimens — wire gold, leaf gold, and crystalline masses — some of which were sold directly to collectors at a premium over bullion value. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. Immigrant miners from Bohemia, Scotland, Wales, and Cornwall brought specific craft knowledge — the Welsh for their understanding of timbering, the Cornish for deep-shaft methods — that shaped the mine's technical character. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. By final closure the mine had yielded $143,154 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. County historical records and a collection of mine logbooks preserved at the state historical society give researchers unusually detailed information about this property's operational history. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkhorn Bullion Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1904 on a hill already pocked with the shallow pits of earlier searchers, the Elkhorn Bullion Mine went deeper and richer than any of its predecessors. The oxidized zone proved exceptionally rich, but the transition to primary sulfide ore at the water table required the company to redesign its milling circuit at significant cost. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. The mine's Chinese workers, hired for surface and timbering tasks at wages below those of white miners, formed a self-contained community at the edge of camp and maintained ties to a Chinese-operated store in the nearest town. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. By final closure the mine had yielded $82,154 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkhorn Chief Mine
mines.description: An experienced drift miner who had spent fifteen years in the California camps identified the ore structure at this site in 1887 and organized the Elkhorn Chief Mine with a handful of trusted partners. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. The boarding house accommodated over a hundred men, feeding them three shifts worth of meals and serving as the social center of the camp through long winter evenings. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. Veterans of the Comstock, the California camps, and the Colorado silver fields found their way here, contributing deep practical knowledge to a management that was often long on capital and short on mining experience. A granite porphyry intrusion had baked the surrounding sedimentary rocks into a resistant hornfels, and the skarn contact between the two formations proved to be the most productive ore zone in the district. Scale was limited by the narrow width of the ore body; the mine could not employ the large crews and heavy equipment that dominated the district's major operations. The mine's cumulative output of $51,893 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The site has been partially reclaimed; the tailings were graded and seeded, the shaft capped with concrete, and the most hazardous open workings fenced — though the outline of the camp is still unmistakable from above. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkhorn Crown Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1894 on a hill already pocked with the shallow pits of earlier searchers, the Elkhorn Crown Mine went deeper and richer than any of its predecessors. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. Two parallel veins converged at depth into a single rich structure, a geological geometry that the mine's resident engineer had predicted on theoretical grounds but proved only after years of systematic development. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. By final closure the mine had yielded $31,082 estimated historical value in gross mineral value, funding the careers of several generations of district miners. Aerial photographs reveal the full extent of the underground workings through surface subsidence patterns — shallow depressions that trace the main vein system for nearly half a mile across the hillside. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkhorn Eagle Mine
mines.description: A party of Welsh hard-rock miners who arrived in 1890 recognized the geology at once — the pyritized schist, the carbonate alteration — and staked the Elkhorn Eagle Mine before the week was out. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. Underground conditions were hard: summer heat in the lower levels, constant moisture, the acrid smell of blasting powder, and ever-present silica dust that shortened miners' lives and left many in later years disabled by silicosis. The vein system was part of a larger epithermal gold-silver network that geologists now understand as a single hydrothermal cell; at the time, miners just followed the ore wherever it led. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. The mine's cumulative output of $86,862 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Preservation efforts have protected the most significant surface structures; the mine is listed on state and national historic registers and continues to attract researchers studying the industrial archaeology of western mining. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkhorn Gold Mine
mines.description: The Elkhorn Gold Mine opened in 1902 after a grizzled veteran spotted the telltale iron-stained gossan capping a rich lode — the kind of surface sign that experienced eyes never miss. High-grade pockets produced spectacular specimens — wire gold, leaf gold, and crystalline masses — some of which were sold directly to collectors at a premium over bullion value. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. Labor relations were tense through much of the mine's life; the Western Federation of Miners organized the workforce in the early twentieth century, and a series of strikes tested management's resolve. A granite porphyry intrusion had baked the surrounding sedimentary rocks into a resistant hornfels, and the skarn contact between the two formations proved to be the most productive ore zone in the district. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. Production records show $60,719 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. County historical records and a collection of mine logbooks preserved at the state historical society give researchers unusually detailed information about this property's operational history. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkhorn King Mine
mines.description: A party of Welsh hard-rock miners who arrived in 1872 recognized the geology at once — the pyritized schist, the carbonate alteration — and staked the Elkhorn King Mine before the week was out. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. The mine superintendent's house — larger and better furnished than the miners' cabins — stood on a slight rise above the camp, visible evidence of the social hierarchy that organized mining communities. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. This was a pocket mine — small scale, high grade, and dependent on the periodic discovery of rich ore shoots to sustain profitability. Production records show $130,897 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkhorn Queen Mine
mines.description: Organized in 1899 by a stock company formed in the state capital, the Elkhorn Queen Mine quickly justified its investors' faith when the first deep level revealed ore far richer than the near-surface sampling had suggested. Assay values in the upper levels ran surprisingly rich in free gold, allowing the operators to ship direct to the smelter without on-site milling during the first years. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. The mine's Chinese workers, hired for surface and timbering tasks at wages below those of white miners, formed a self-contained community at the edge of camp and maintained ties to a Chinese-operated store in the nearest town. A stock promotion scandal in the mine's middle years drove out the original investors and brought in new management, which proved to be an inadvertent improvement: the new superintendent was a better practical miner than his predecessor. With a workforce that rarely exceeded fifteen men, the mine was nonetheless a going concern for years, producing consistently if not spectacularly. The mine's cumulative output of $41,742 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. Little remains above ground: the concrete foundation of the shaft house, the long gray ribbon of tailings, and a few ore-bin timbers slowly returning to the soil. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkhorn Silver Mine
mines.description: Winter did not stop the founding of the Elkhorn Silver Mine; in 1888, men working frozen ground with picks and bars broke into a gold-bearing structure that would define the next two decades. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. Underground conditions were hard: summer heat in the lower levels, constant moisture, the acrid smell of blasting powder, and ever-present silica dust that shortened miners' lives and left many in later years disabled by silicosis. The deposit was zoned: near-surface gold grading into silver-dominant ore at depth, with lead-zinc values increasing in the lowest levels — a telescoped epithermal system that geologists found instructive. Scale was limited by the narrow width of the ore body; the mine could not employ the large crews and heavy equipment that dominated the district's major operations. The mine's cumulative output of $115,939 estimated historical value represented a solid return on the capital invested, though not the bonanza the original promoters had promised. The region's mining heritage tourism industry has found the Elkhorn Silver Mine site to be one of its most evocative stops, combining accessible ruins with a well-documented historical record. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Elkhorn Star Mine
mines.description: Brothers who had worked the Comstock bonanzas for a decade brought their savings and their skills to this district in 1897 and founded the Elkhorn Star Mine on a vein they traced across three separate outcroppings. Free gold could be seen with the naked eye in the richest stopes, and careful mucking by experienced miners salvaged small pockets that would have been lost in a less attentive operation. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. The mine's machine shop could fabricate most replacement parts on-site, reducing the costly delays that plagued more remote operations dependent on long supply chains. A union organizer arrived in the spring of the mine's busiest year and spent months carefully building a membership base before calling the strike that ultimately improved wage rates across the whole district. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. The property never attracted the capital needed for large-scale development and remained a small, family-run operation through most of its productive life. Production records show $70,742 estimated historical value in recovered mineral value — a respectable figure that sustained operations for more than a decade. The camp once bustled with the noise of stamps, drills, and hoists; today it is silent except for the wind, the old camp roads slowly disappearing under encroaching vegetation. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Atlantic Mine
mines.description: The Atlantic Mine was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1892 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the South Pass district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. Total recorded output reached $18,837 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Atlantic Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Carissa Mine
mines.description: The Carissa Mine came to light when a lone placer miner noticed the gleam of native gold in the creek gravel, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1902 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the South Pass district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. Total recorded output reached $29,599 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Carissa Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Duncan Mine
mines.description: The Duncan Mine came to notice when a surveying party camped on the hillside and examined the local float rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1902 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the South Pass district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. Total recorded output reached $36,645 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Duncan Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Emma Mine
mines.description: The Emma Mine was discovered by a group of forty-niners following the classic miner's instinct—pick up every likely rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1891 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the South Pass district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. Total recorded output reached $52,819 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Emma Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Ester Mine
mines.description: The Ester Mine came to light when a lone placer miner noticed the gleam of native gold in the creek gravel, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1902 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the South Pass district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. Total recorded output reached $35,979 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Ester Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Flagstaff Mine
mines.description: The Flagstaff Mine was staked after local ranchers found suspicious yellow glints in a dry wash near their pasture, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1906 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the South Pass district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. Total recorded output reached $66,030 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Flagstaff Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Fortune Mine
mines.description: The Fortune Mine emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1874 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the South Pass district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. Total recorded output reached $24,650 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Fortune Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Harvey Mine
mines.description: The Harvey Mine was first staked by prospectors who traced a promising quartz vein up the mountain drainage, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1879 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the South Pass district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. Total recorded output reached $60,185 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Harvey Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.

mines.name: Last Chance Mine
mines.description: The Last Chance Mine was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1912 amid the rush and optimism of the Progressive Era. Located in the South Pass district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. Total recorded output reached $39,978 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Last Chance Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Little Soldier Mine
mines.description: The Little Soldier Mine was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1897 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the South Pass district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. Total recorded output reached $53,950 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Little Soldier Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Nevada Mine
mines.description: The Nevada Mine was pegged when a winter camp cook noticed sparkling flecks in the rock used for his fireplace, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1899 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the South Pass district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. Total recorded output reached $25,484 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Nevada Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: North American Mine
mines.description: The North American Mine came to light when a lone placer miner noticed the gleam of native gold in the creek gravel, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1906 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the South Pass district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. Total recorded output reached $38,890 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the North American Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Oregon Buttes Mine
mines.description: The Oregon Buttes Mine was pegged when a winter camp cook noticed sparkling flecks in the rock used for his fireplace, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1892 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the South Pass district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. Total recorded output reached $89,296 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Oregon Buttes Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM
