mines.name: Atlantic City Mine
mines.description: The Atlantic City Mine came to light when a lone placer miner noticed the gleam of native gold in the creek gravel, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1900 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Atlantic City district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. Total recorded output reached $61,697 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Atlantic City Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Buckeye Mine
mines.description: The Buckeye Mine came to notice when a surveying party camped on the hillside and examined the local float rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1902 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Atlantic City district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. Total recorded output reached $41,391 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Buckeye Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Burr Mine
mines.description: The Burr Mine was identified by a government geologist who recognized the telltale iron gossan capping a rich lode, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1904 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Atlantic City district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. Total recorded output reached $29,987 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Burr Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Continental Mine
mines.description: The Continental Mine emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1869 amid the rush and optimism of the Civil War era. Located in the Atlantic City district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. Total recorded output reached $34,101 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Continental Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Iron Ore Mine
mines.description: The Iron Ore Mine was identified by a government geologist who recognized the telltale iron gossan capping a rich lode, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1871 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Atlantic City district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Rock from the lower levels ran so rich that hand-sorted high-grade ore was shipped directly to the smelter without milling. Smallpox swept through the miners' camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and slowing ore production for weeks. The mine's foreman kept meticulous logbooks that survive today, recording every shift's progress, accidents, and unusual ore encounters in careful copperplate. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. Total recorded output reached $47,011 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Iron Ore Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Lincoln Mine
mines.description: The Lincoln Mine was uncovered by a former sea captain who had traded his ship's compass for a miner's pick, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1907 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Atlantic City district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. Total recorded output reached $58,476 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Lincoln Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.

mines.name: Grand Encampment Mine
mines.description: The Grand Encampment Mine was discovered by a veteran Cornish miner who said the ground smelled like the old copper country at home, launching copper mining operations in 1899 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Encampment district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. Total recorded output reached $53,037 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Grand Encampment Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.

mines.name: Keystone Mine
mines.description: The Keystone Mine was located by prospectors following old reports of 'green rocks' used by indigenous peoples as paint pigment, launching copper mining operations in 1904 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Encampment district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. Total recorded output reached $89,825 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Keystone Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: North American Mine
mines.description: The North American Mine was identified by its brilliant blue-green gossan, visible from the valley floor, launching copper mining operations in 1908 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Encampment district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. Total recorded output reached $78,192 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the North American Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Rudefeha Mine

The operation employed hundreds of men at its height, filling a nearby camp with boarding houses, saloons, and a school for miners' children. A runaway ore skip in the main shaft killed two men and injured four others, prompting the installation of new safety catches on all hoists. The owner, a self-made man who had arrived with nothing but a grubstake and an iron will, became one of the wealthiest citizens in the territory. The main shaft was timbered with massive square sets, each joint hand-fitted by skilled carpenters, creating a cathedral-like underground gallery. Total recorded output reached $64,871 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Rudefeha Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The shaft is capped and fenced for safety, but hikers can still trace the ore-cart rails to the edge of the old mill site.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Centennial Gold Mine
mines.description: The Centennial Gold Mine was staked after local ranchers found suspicious yellow glints in a dry wash near their pasture, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1906 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Centennial district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. During its busiest years the mine ran three full shifts, with a steady stream of ore wagons rumbling down the canyon road at all hours. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. Total recorded output reached $24,815 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Centennial Gold Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and scattered machinery form an open-air industrial museum on BLM land near the old camp site.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Centennial Eagle Mine
mines.description: The Centennial Eagle Mine was identified by a government geologist who recognized the telltale iron gossan capping a rich lode, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1897 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Two separate ore bodies were eventually found to merge at depth, proving the mine's long-term viability and unlocking a new phase of development. Total recorded output reached $25,481 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Centennial Eagle Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Today the site is accessible via a Forest Service road and draws curious visitors who pick through the tailings for mineral samples.

mines.name: Centennial Star Mine
mines.description: The Centennial Star Mine was located after a chance surface float of gold-bearing quartz caught a prospector's eye, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1892 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the Centennial district of Wyoming, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company imported the latest Cornish pump technology to battle the underground water that threatened to drown the deeper workings. Persistent underground flooding in the lower levels prompted the costly installation of a Cornish pump that became the largest in the district. A charismatic labor organizer arrived one summer and spent months quietly signing up men before calling a strike that shut the mine for weeks. The mill sat in the valley below, its stamp batteries rattling day and night and its tailings pond spreading across the old floodplain. Total recorded output reached $27,522 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Centennial Star Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Rockhounds visit seasonally, working through the tailings for fragments of the original ore: chalcopyrite, pyrite, quartz crystals, and native metal.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - USFS

mines.name: Atlanta Mine
mines.description: The Atlanta Mine emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1874 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Idaho City district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At its zenith the mine supported an entire community: assay office, machine shop, blacksmith, carpenter shop, and company store. The infamous hard-rock miners' strike of the era brought armed guards and Pinkerton detectives to the property, souring labor relations for years. Women ran boarding houses and laundries on the town's main street, forming an economic backbone that outlasted several booms and busts. The upper levels intersected a large underground lake that required constant pumping to keep the deep workings from flooding. Total recorded output reached $168,601 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Atlanta Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The land has been partially reclaimed, but the scarred hillside and flat tailings terrace are unmistakable markers of the mine's industrial past.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Boulder Creek Mine
mines.description: The Boulder Creek Mine emerged from the search of hardened veterans who had already worked out a dozen smaller claims, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1876 amid the rush and optimism of the post-war silver boom. Located in the Idaho City district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Drilling records fell repeatedly as miners pushed deeper, guided by candle-flame and the nose of experienced shift bosses. An early-morning powder explosion rocked the shaft house, shattering windows in the camp a quarter-mile away and briefly paralyzing operations. The company doctor, on call around the clock, stitched wounds and set bones in a crude surgery behind the shaft house. An aerial tramway carried buckets of ore from the portal to the mill, its cables and towers stretching dramatically across a quarter-mile of canyon. Total recorded output reached $166,223 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Boulder Creek Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A nearby ghost town—just a scattering of cabin foundations and a collapsed false-front storefront—is a companion reminder of the mining era.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Chatcolet Mine
mines.description: The Chatcolet Mine was discovered by a group of forty-niners following the classic miner's instinct—pick up every likely rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1907 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Idaho City district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. At peak output the headframe ran day and night; the stamp mill's rhythmic thunder could be heard ten miles away across the valley. Labor unrest in the early twentieth century brought in the Western Federation of Miners, and a bitter strike closed the mine for nearly a year. A tight-knit community of Slavic immigrants, many recruited directly from Bohemian and Croatian mining villages, formed the backbone of the labor force. Three separate shafts served different levels, connected by a network of raises and manways that allowed workers to move through the mine safely. Total recorded output reached $56,092 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Chatcolet Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. A state historic park now preserves the surface structures, operating a small visitor center and offering summer tours of the mine yard.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Clear Creek Mine
mines.description: The Clear Creek Mine was discovered by a group of forty-niners following the classic miner's instinct—pick up every likely rock, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1907 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the Idaho City district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. Total recorded output reached $98,350 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Clear Creek Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.

mines.name: Kaiser Mine

Located in the Idaho City district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. Total recorded output reached $123,396 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Kaiser Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

mines.name: Pioneerville Mine
mines.description: The Pioneerville Mine was identified by a government geologist who recognized the telltale iron gossan capping a rich lode, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1868 amid the rush and optimism of the Civil War era. Located in the Idaho City district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. A flash flood in the mill tailings pond in the late boom years forced a month-long shutdown and costly repairs to the crushing plant. The mine's Chinese workers, barred from the skilled drilling crews, excelled at the dangerous work of mucking and timbering the deeper levels. The ore body proved wider at depth than at the surface, encouraging ever-deeper sinking and driving the shaft to record levels. Total recorded output reached $117,352 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Pioneerville Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. mines.name: Black Jack Mine

Located in the Silver City district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. Total recorded output reached $73,071 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Black Jack Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. mines.name: Booneville Mine
mines.description: The Booneville Mine emerged when a freighter's mule kicked loose a chunk of ore that assayed three hundred ounces silver per ton, launching silver mining operations in 1866 amid the rush and optimism of the Civil War era. Located in the Silver City district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Management secured contracts to supply a regional smelter, locking in favorable prices and driving expansion of the underground workings. A price collapse on world markets forced a temporary shutdown that lasted two years, scattering the workforce across the region. Seasoned veterans who had worked every major camp in the West ended up here, drawn by the reputation of the ore body. Miners sank the main shaft to depths exceeding a thousand feet, honeycombing the mountain with stopes and connecting drifts. Total recorded output reached $150,570 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Booneville Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The headframe and ore bins remain standing, weather-beaten but intact, managed by a local historical society that leads occasional tours.

mines.name: Gold Finch Mine
mines.description: The Gold Finch Mine was located after a chance surface float of gold-bearing quartz caught a prospector's eye, launching hard-rock gold mining operations in 1864 amid the rush and optimism of the Civil War era. Located in the Warren district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. Output climbed year after year through the boom decade, funding fine homes, a brick bank, and a narrow-gauge railroad spur to the mill. A dynamite misfire in the deepest stope caused a partial collapse that required months of careful timbering before work could resume. Families settled nearby, filling a small town with a church, a one-room school, and a lively Independence Day celebration that drew miners from miles around. The surface plant grew over the decades to include a large headframe, ore bins, a rock crusher, a flotation mill, and a leaching yard. Total recorded output reached $83,492 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Gold Finch Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. The site is listed on the National Register of Historic Places, ensuring basic preservation of the surviving surface features.

mines.name: De Lamar Silver Mine
mines.description: The De Lamar Silver Mine was traced to outcropping gray carbonate ore that old-timers called 'black silver', launching silver mining operations in 1908 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the De Lamar district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. An on-site reduction plant was erected to process lower-grade material, squeezing every dollar of value from the ore body. A catastrophic cave-in in one of the peak years trapped a dozen men underground for three days before a rescue drift broke through. The workforce was a polyglot mix of Irish, Welsh, Scandinavian, and Chinese laborers, each group occupying its own corner of the sprawling camp. Underground temperatures climbed dangerously in the deeper workings, forcing the company to improve ventilation and supply ice water to drilling crews. Total recorded output reached $74,330 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the De Lamar Silver Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Bureau of Land Management maintains public access to the surface workings; the main shaft is gated but visible from the edge of the collar.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Oro Fino Mine
mines.description: The Oro Fino Mine was traced to outcropping gray carbonate ore that old-timers called 'black silver', launching silver mining operations in 1903 amid the rush and optimism of the early twentieth century. Located in the De Lamar district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. In its prime the property was valued in the millions and attracted investors from as far as London and New York. A fire in the timber sets of the upper level destroyed several hundred feet of drift and set production back by an entire season. The mine superintendent was a hard-driving Scotsman famous for pushing men and machines beyond their rated limits. A decline adit near the base of the ridge drained the lower workings and doubled as a haulage way for loaded ore cars. Total recorded output reached $108,926 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Oro Fino Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. Little remains above ground beyond the concrete foundations of the shaft house and the long gray ribbon of tailings across the hillside.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Silver Butte Mine
mines.description: The Silver Butte Mine came to light after a storm washed away hillside debris and exposed a wide streak of galena, launching silver mining operations in 1892 amid the rush and optimism of the final frontier boom. Located in the De Lamar district of Idaho, the property quickly attracted seasoned prospectors and Eastern capital alike. The company sank a second shaft to ventilate the rapidly deepening stopes and allow faster removal of broken ore. The discovery of a rich new ore shoot in the lower levels reversed the mine's fortunes just as the owners were considering closure. Cornish hard-rock miners, known locally as 'Cousin Jacks,' dominated the skilled workforce and brought their traditional mining knowledge from Cornwall. Miles of lateral drifts radiated outward from the main shaft, following the irregular ore body through hard country rock. Total recorded output reached $105,106 estimated historical value. By the time operations wound down, the Silver Butte Mine had left an indelible mark on the region's economic and cultural landscape. An interpretive trail now winds through the mine yard, where plaques describe the daily life of miners who once filled these now-silent buildings.
mines.source: USGS Mineral Resources Data System - BLM

mines.name: Trade Dollar Mine
